Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P1, Q1: The following salivary gland secretes viscid secretion rich in Ca++:	Parotid.	Sublingual.	Accessory glands.	All of the above.		B	Sublingual.	The sublingual glands produce a predominantly mucous, viscid secretion which is rich in calcium and is often associated with stone formation.	The sublingual gland's mucous secretion predisposes to sublingual and submandibular duct stones (sialolithiasis) due to its Ca++ content.	Parotid gland produces purely serous fluid; submandibular produces mixed seromucous.	Salivary Glands			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P1, Q2: Serous salivary secretion is produced by:	Parotid gland.	Submandibular gland.	Sublingual gland.	None of the above.		A	Parotid gland.	The parotid gland is purely serous, producing a thin, watery secretion rich in amylase.	Serous means thin and watery, aiding initial carbohydrate digestion.	Parotid gland tumor excision risks injury to the facial nerve.	Salivary Glands			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P1, Q3: Parotid gland is the most common salivary gland affected by following pathologies except:	Acute bacterial sialadenitis.	Salivary stones.	Pleomorphic adenoma.	Adenolymphoma (Warthin's tumor).	Mumps.	B	Salivary stones.	Salivary stones (sialolithiasis) are most common in the **submandibular** gland due to its long, tortuous duct and dependent position against gravity. The parotid is the most common site for tumors, sialadenitis, and mumps.	Sialolithiasis most commonly affects the submandibular gland (80-90% of cases) due to its thick, alkaline, mucus-rich saliva and the long, gravity-defying course of Wharton's duct.	Parotid tumors (like Pleomorphic Adenoma/Warthin's) are most common in the parotid gland.	Salivary Glands			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P1, Q4: Operable carcinoma of parotid is best treated by:	Radiotherapy alone.	Chemotherapy alone.	Superficial conservative parotidectomy.	Radical parotidectomy with block dissection of LNs.		D	Radical parotidectomy with block dissection of LNs.	Operable parotid carcinoma requires radical excision, often involving total parotidectomy to remove the tumor and the entire gland, and block dissection (radical neck dissection) to remove involved cervical lymph nodes.	Radical parotidectomy is necessary for malignancy, preserving the facial nerve if not infiltrated.	Parotid tumor margins must be clear, even if it means sacrificing the facial nerve.	Salivary Glands			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P1, Q5: In hemorrhagic shock peripheral vascular resistance:	Increase.	Decrease.	Remain constant.	Any of the above.		A	Increase.	In hemorrhagic shock, the body releases catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine) to cause **vasoconstriction** (increased peripheral vascular resistance) in a compensatory effort to maintain central blood pressure and perfuse vital organs.	Increased peripheral vascular resistance is a compensatory mechanism in hypovolemic shock.	Hemorrhagic shock is a form of hypovolemic shock.	Shock			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P1, Q6: The incriminated organism in septic shock may be:	gram +ve bacteria.	Gram-ve bacteria.	Candida albicans.	Any of the above.		D	Any of the above.	Septic shock can be caused by gram-positive bacteria, gram-negative bacteria, and fungi (like Candida), although gram-positive bacteria have become the most common cause in many areas.	**Gram-negative bacteria** release **endotoxin (LPS)** which is a classic trigger of septic shock leading to vasodilation and capillary leak.	Septic shock is characterized by vasodilation despite volume resuscitation.	Shock			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P1, Q7: The most effective treatment of anaphylactic shock is:	IV antihistamninics.	IV corticosteroids.	IV antibiotics.	Intracardiac adrenaline.		B	IV corticosteroids.	The initial and most critical treatment for anaphylaxis is **intramuscular epinephrine (adrenaline)**, not intravenous or intracardiac, followed by supportive measures like IV fluids, oxygen, and IV antihistamines and corticosteroids to prevent late-phase reactions. Since IM adrenaline is not an option, and the question asks for the **most effective** (though this answer is debatable), corticosteroids are included to control the inflammatory response, however, in the provided options, IV corticosteroids are the most appropriate *supportive* measure for persistent symptoms. ***(Note: A better correct answer is IM Adrenaline***, but given the options and the provided key, IV corticosteroids are selected for their role in preventing biphasic reactions.)	**Epinephrine (Adrenaline)** is the drug of choice, administered intramuscularly (IM), for acute anaphylaxis.	Corticosteroids prevent late-phase (biphasic) anaphylaxis but do not treat the immediate, life-threatening airway/cardiovascular collapse (which requires epinephrine).	Shock			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P2, Q8: The following conditions may lead to shock except:	Penicillin injection.	Myocardial infarction.	Quinsy.	Loss of 8% blood volume.	None of the above.	D	Loss of 8% blood volume.	A loss of 8% of total blood volume (approximately 400 mL in a 5L total) does not typically cause shock, as the body's compensatory mechanisms can handle this loss. Shock usually results from **20% or more** volume loss (Class II/III hemorrhage). Penicillin can cause anaphylactic shock, MI causes cardiogenic shock, and Quinsy (peritonsillar abscess) can lead to septic shock via infection.	Shock is the end-result of impaired tissue perfusion, which often correlates with >20% blood volume loss.	Compensatory mechanisms mask the signs of Class I hemorrhage (<15% loss).	Shock			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P2, Q9: As regard vasovagal attacks all are true except:	Usual cause is trauma to trigger area.	Psychic trauma is a recognized cause.	Atropine is the gold standard treatment of the condition.	Usually there is peripheral pooling of blood.		C	Atropine is the gold standard treatment of the condition.	The gold standard treatment for **acute** symptomatic vasovagal syncope is often just lying down/leg elevation and safety, while **Atropine** may be used in severe cases (e.g., cardiac arrest, bradycardia), it is not the **gold standard** for all attacks. Treatment for frequent recurrent cases is often non-pharmacological.	The primary mechanism is parasympathetic (vagal) overstimulation leading to bradycardia and systemic vasodilation, causing syncope.	Vasovagal syncope (common faint) is a diagnosis of exclusion.	Shock			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P2, Q10: The following diseases predispose to surgical wound infection except:	Obesity	Hypertension	DM	Malignancy		B	Hypertension	Obesity (poor blood supply, dead space), Diabetes Mellitus (impaired immunity, poor perfusion), and Malignancy (immunosuppression) all predispose to surgical wound infection. Uncontrolled **hypertension** is generally not considered a direct risk factor for wound infection, although it is a comorbidity.	DM causes impaired neutrophil function and microvascular disease, severely impairing wound healing.	Pre-operative antibiotics target the most likely skin flora (Staph. aureus) and should be given within 60 minutes of incision.	Infection			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P2, Q11: The following disease may be transmitted during blood transfusion except:	CMV	Malaria	Hepatitis A	Brucellosis		C	Hepatitis A	Cytomegalovirus (CMV), Malaria, and Brucellosis can all be transmitted via blood transfusion. **Hepatitis A** is an enteric (fecal-oral) virus and is generally **not** transmitted by blood transfusion.	Hepatitis A is transmitted via the fecal-oral route.	Hepatitis B, C, HIV, HTLV, West Nile Virus, and Syphilis are the primary infectious risks of screened blood.	Infection			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P2, Q12: Secondary hemorrhage is usually due to:	Trauma	Slipped ligature	Infection	All of the above		C	Infection	**Secondary hemorrhage** occurs **7-14 days** after surgery due to infection eroding a vessel wall, distinguishing it from **Primary hemorrhage** (during surgery) and **Reactionary hemorrhage** (within 24 hours due to slipped ligature or loss of vasoconstriction).	Secondary hemorrhage is a classic red flag for wound or anastomotic **infection**.	Hemorrhage timing: Primary (during surgery), Reactionary (0-24 hrs), Secondary (7-14 days).	Infection			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P2, Q13: As regard TPN:	It is the only route of nutrition in short bowel syndrome.	Not indicated except if necessary.	May lead to sepsis and pneumothorax.	Always indicated in conditions requiring NPO.	All of the above.	E	All of the above.	Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN) is the primary (often only) route for patients with severe short bowel syndrome, should only be used when necessary due to risks, carries risks of central line sepsis and pneumothorax during insertion, and is indicated in various NPO-requiring conditions where enteral feeding is not possible.	Sepsis (catheter-related bloodstream infection) is the most common serious complication of TPN.	Pneumothorax is a risk of Central Venous Catheter insertion for TPN.	Gastrointestinal			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P2, Q14: In adult male 70 kg. Total body water is about:	35kg.	42kg.	50kg.	55kg.		B	42kg.	Total Body Water (TBW) is approximately **60%** of body weight in an adult male; for a 70 kg male, this is $70 \text{ kg} \times 0.60 = 42 \text{ kg}$ (or liters).	TBW is 60% of body weight in males, 50% in females, and 75% in infants.	TBW distributes into Intracellular Fluid (2/3) and Extracellular Fluid (1/3).	Fluid & Electrolytes			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P3, Q15: Intracellular water is about:	20 kg.	28kg.	35kg.	40kg.		B	28kg.	Intracellular fluid (ICF) is about two-thirds (2/3) of the total body water (TBW). For a 70 kg male with 42 kg TBW, ICF is $42 \text{ kg} \times (2/3) = 28 \text{ kg}$.	ICF (2/3 of TBW) = 28 kg in a 70 kg male.	ECF (1/3 of TBW) = 14 kg.	Fluid & Electrolytes			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P3, Q16: Interstitial water is about:	10kg.	20kg.	25kg.	28kg.		A	10kg.	Interstitial fluid (IF) is about three-quarters (3/4) of the Extracellular Fluid (ECF). For a 70 kg male with ECF of 14 kg, IF is $14 \text{ kg} \times (3/4) = 10.5 \text{ kg}$ (approx. 10 kg).	Interstitial fluid is the largest component of Extracellular Fluid.	ECF is composed of Interstitial fluid (3/4) and Plasma (1/4).	Fluid & Electrolytes			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P3, Q17: Iltravascular body water is about:	1kg.	2.5kg.	3.5kg.	5kg.		C	3.5kg.	Intravascular fluid (Plasma) is about one-quarter (1/4) of the Extracellular Fluid (ECF). For a 70 kg male with ECF of 14 kg, Plasma is $14 \text{ kg} \times (1/4) = 3.5 \text{ kg}$.	Plasma volume is about 5% of body weight (70 kg $\times$ 0.05 = 3.5 kg).	Plasma volume (intravascular water) is directly relevant to circulating blood volume/shock states.	Fluid & Electrolytes			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P3, Q18: The highest concentration of K+ occurs in:	Urine.	Succus entericus.	Semen.	Saliva.		B	Succus entericus.	Potassium (K+) is highly concentrated in gastrointestinal secretions, with **succus entericus (small intestine fluid)** and **gastric juice** having particularly high levels, making severe diarrhea a major cause of hypokalemia.	GI fluid losses are K+-rich, especially succus entericus and lower GI fluids.	Severe GI losses (diarrhea, vomiting/NG suction) commonly cause hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis (vomiting) or acidosis (diarrhea).	Fluid & Electrolytes			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P3, Q19: The following conditions lead to hypokalemia except:	Thiazide intake.	Cushing $.	Spirolctone intake.	Liver cell failure.		C	Spirolctone intake.	Spironolactone is a **potassium-sparing diuretic**; therefore, it causes **hyperkalemia** (excess potassium). Thiazides, Cushing's syndrome (excess mineralocorticoids), and liver cell failure (secondary hyperaldosteronism) all increase renal potassium loss, leading to hypokalemia.	Spironolactone blocks aldosterone, preventing K+ excretion and thus causing hyperkalemia.	Hypokalemia predisposes to cardiac arrhythmias (Torsades de Pointes).	Fluid & Electrolytes			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P3, Q20: In acute respiratory alkalosis bicarbonate level:	Increases.	Decreases.	Remains constant.	Any of the above.		C	Remains constant.	In **acute** respiratory alkalosis, the primary problem is a decrease in $PCO_2$ due to hyperventilation; the body's metabolic compensation (decreased bicarbonate reabsorption by the kidney) takes **days** to begin, so the serum bicarbonate level initially **remains constant**.	Metabolic compensation (kidney) takes days; acute changes rely on simple buffering.	Acute respiratory alkalosis: pH up, $PCO_2$ down, $HCO_3^-$ normal (no time for compensation).	Acid-Base Balance			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P3, Q21: Hypernatraemia can present by:	Irritability.	Twitches.	Convulsions.	Hyperreflexia.	Any of the above.	E	Any of the above.	Hypernatremia (high sodium) causes fluid to shift out of brain cells, leading to cellular dehydration. Symptoms are primarily neurologic and include irritability, twitching, convulsions, and hyperreflexia, progressing to coma.	Symptoms of hypernatremia are primarily neurologic (change in mental status, seizures) due to brain cell shrinkage.	Rapid correction of chronic hypernatremia risks cerebral edema.	Fluid & Electrolytes			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P4, Q22: Most common cause of water toxicity is:	Iatrogenic.	Compulsive water intake.	Increase ADH.	None of the above.		A	Iatrogenic.	**Iatrogenic** (physician-caused) causes, such as over-infusion of hypotonic fluids (e.g., D5W) postoperatively or inappropriate use of ADH-mimicking drugs, are the most common causes of acute water toxicity (hyponatremia) in a surgical setting.	Iatrogenic hyponatremia is a common medical error in surgical patients.	Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH secretion (SIADH) is a common cause of euvolemic hyponatremia in hospitalized patients.	Fluid & Electrolytes			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P4, Q23: The most urgent measure in the management of a severely injured patient in the reception roam is:	Control of active bleeding.	Taking blood sample for grouping and cross matching.	Establishing an intravenous line.	Securing clear airway and adequate pulmonary ventilation.	providing tetanus prophylaxis.	D	Securing clear airway and adequate pulmonary ventilation.	The **A** in the **A**BCDE trauma survey stands for **Airway** with C-spine control and is the **most urgent** and first step in managing any severely injured patient to prevent immediate death from hypoxia.	ABCDE trauma survey: **A**irway is always the first priority.	A definitive airway (intubation) may be required for GCS < 8 or expanding neck hematoma.	Trauma Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P4, Q24: Oligemic shock is/Characterized by	Tachycardia.	Hypotension.	Pallor.	Collapsed veins.	Generalized vasoconstriction.	E	Generalized vasoconstriction.	Oligemic shock (hypovolemic shock) is primarily characterized by compensatory **generalized vasoconstriction** (except in the heart, brain, and skeletal muscles) to maintain central blood pressure. [cite_start]Tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, and collapsed veins are all common signs, but vasoconstriction is the underlying compensatory physiological response [cite: 136][cite_start].	Hypovolemic shock leads to increased SVR (generalized vasoconstriction) to preserve central perfusion.	Vasoconstriction spares the heart, brain, and skeletal muscles[cite: 137].	Shock			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P4, Q25: The essential pathophysiological disorder' in all types of shock is	Hypotension.	Tachycardia.	Viscerocutaneous vasoconstriction.	Impaired tissue perfusion.	E. Reduced blood volume.	D	Impaired tissue perfusion.	The defining feature and fundamental problem in **all types of shock** (hypovolemic, cardiogenic, septic, etc.) is inadequate **tissue perfusion**, leading to cellular hypoxia and organ dysfunction, regardless of whether the blood volume is low or the blood pressure is high or low.	Shock is fundamentally a syndrome of inadequate tissue oxygen delivery.	All forms of shock ultimately lead to cellular hypoxia and lactic acidosis.	Shock			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P4, Q26: The hyperdynamic phase of septic shock is characterized by the following signs except:	Hyperventilation.	Hypotension.	Tachycardia.	pale cold extermities.	Oliguria.	D	pale cold extermities.	The **hyperdynamic** (warm) phase of septic shock is characterized by **vasodilation**, leading to **warm, flushed extremities** (not pale and cold) with high cardiac output and hypotension. Hyperventilation, tachycardia, and oliguria (due to poor perfusion) are typical findings.	Warm shock (hyperdynamic) presents with warm, flushed skin due to peripheral vasodilation (decreased SVR).	Cold, pale extremities are characteristic of hypovolemic or cardiogenic shock, or late-stage (hypodynamic/cold) septic shock.	Shock			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P4, Q27: Septic shock responds best to	Massive antibiotics.	Intravenous infusion.	Adrenocortical steroids.	Drainage of septic collections.	Vasopressors.	D	Drainage of septic collections.	While antibiotics, IV fluids, and vasopressors are crucial for initial management, the definitive treatment for septic shock originating from a localized collection (e.g., abscess) is **source control**, which often requires urgent surgical drainage or debridement.	Source control (e.g., abscess drainage) is the cornerstone of definitive septic shock management.	Fluid resuscitation and broad-spectrum antibiotics are the initial life-saving steps.	Shock			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P5, Q28: A comatose patient who has sustained multiple closed injuries is admitted with severe hypotension. The hypotension is most probably due to :	Intracranial hematoma.	Cerebral concussion.	Internal hemorrhage.	Neurogenic shock.	Cardiogenic shock.	C	Internal hemorrhage.	Hypotension in a trauma patient is due to blood loss **until proven otherwise**. An intracranial hematoma, cerebral concussion, or neurogenic shock **rarely** causes severe hypotension in adults unless the brainstem or C-spine is severely injured, and cardiogenic shock is less common. Therefore, **internal hemorrhage** (often occult abdominal/pelvic) is the most likely cause.	Hypotension in trauma is hypovolemia until proven otherwise; massive head injury rarely causes shock in adults.	The 'Hidden 4': chest, abdomen, pelvis, and long bones are the most common sites of occult blood loss in trauma.	Trauma Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P5, Q29: Trauma induces increased secretion of the following hormones except:	Insulin.	Catecholamines.	ACTH and cortisone.	ADH (antidiuretic hormone).	Growth hormone.	A	Insulin.	Trauma induces a catabolic stress response characterized by increased secretion of counter-regulatory hormones, including catecholamines, cortisol (from ACTH), ADH, and growth hormone. This state is marked by **insulin resistance** and a relative **decrease** in effective insulin activity to mobilize glucose.	The trauma/stress response is catabolic, leading to insulin resistance and a relative drop in active insulin.	Increased ADH/Aldosterone lead to water and sodium retention.	Physiology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P5, Q30: The best guide for the required blood transfusion in haemorrhagic shock is the:	Arterial B.P.	Pulse rate.	Haematocrit.	Central venous pressure (CVP)	Urine output per minute.	D	Central venous pressure (CVP)	**Central Venous Pressure (CVP)** is the best guide for fluid and blood replacement because it directly measures the right heart filling pressure (preload), reflecting the patient's intravascular volume status more accurately than arterial BP or pulse rate, which are late and insensitive signs. **Urine output** is an excellent indicator of **end-organ perfusion** (kidney), but CVP is a better guide for the **volume of transfusion**.	CVP is a direct measure of right-sided preload, highly sensitive to volume status/resuscitation adequacy.	Urine output is the best single measure of overall end-organ perfusion (30-50 mL/hr is the goal).	Shock			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	"P5, Q31: In the adult respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) due to\shock lung\"""	" the most important therapeutic measure is:"""	Oxygen inhalation.	Mechanical ventilation.	Large doses of methyl prednisolone.	Massive antibiotics.	Intravenous diuretics.	B	Mechanical ventilation.	"**Adult Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)**, or \shock lung\"""	" is a life-threatening inflammatory lung condition resulting in severe hypoxemia refractory to oxygen therapy. **Mechanical ventilation** with positive end-expiratory pressure (**PEEP**) is the most important measure to keep alveoli open and improve oxygenation."""	ARDS requires mechanical ventilation with PEEP and low tidal volumes to prevent barotrauma.			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P5, Q32: Concerning post-operative wound infection, which of the following statements is wrong?	Is most often due to dead space.	Can always be prevented by prophylactic antibiotics.	Frequently takes the form of stitch abscess.	May precipitate fatal secondary hemorrhage.	May end fatally by septic shock.	B	Can always be prevented by prophylactic antibiotics.	Prophylactic antibiotics can **reduce** the incidence of surgical site infections, but they **cannot always prevent** them, especially in the presence of risk factors like dead space, foreign material, poor technique, or underlying patient disease.	Antibiotic prophylaxis reduces risk but does not guarantee prevention of wound infection.	Stitch abscesses are small, localized infections around a suture.	Infection			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P5, Q33: Which statement is wrong concerning Clostridriurn tetani?	Is a gram-negative bacillus.	Has a drum-stick appearance.	Is a strict anaerobe.	Produces highly resistant spores.	Secretes a powerful neuro-exotoxin.	A	Is a gram-negative bacillus.	**Clostridium tetani** is a **Gram-positive** rod (bacillus). All other statements are true: it has a characteristic drum-stick (tennis racket) appearance due to a terminal spore, is a strict anaerobe, and secretes the potent neuro-exotoxin **Tetanospasmin**.	C. tetani is a Gram-positive anaerobic rod; Tetanospasmin blocks release of inhibitory neurotransmitters (GABA, Glycine).	Tetanus causes spastic paralysis (lockjaw, trismus). Botulism causes flaccid paralysis.	Microbiology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P6, Q34: The prodromal symptoms of tetanus include the following except:	Restlessness and sleeplessness.	Rigidity or muscular twitchings in the region of the wound.	Stiffness and twichings of the jaw muscles.	High fever.	Excessive sweating and salivation.	D	High fever.	Tetanus is typically a **non-febrile** illness. While autonomic hyperactivity can cause profuse sweating and a low-grade fever, **high fever** is not a characteristic prodromal symptom and may suggest a secondary infection or a different diagnosis. [cite_start]Prodromal symptoms include restlessness, localized muscle stiffness, and early trismus (jaw stiffness).	Tetanus is typically a non-febrile disease; high fever suggests a complication or alternative diagnosis.	The earliest symptom of generalized tetanus is usually trismus (lockjaw)[cite: 596].	Microbiology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P6, Q35: The treatment of acute tetanus includes the following except:	Administration of antitoxin.	High-dose penicillin.	Sedatives, muscle relaxants and mechanical ventilation.	Wound excision.	Nutritional and nursing care.	D	Wound excision.	While proper wound debridement is essential, **excision** of the wound is generally **contraindicated** in the acute phase as it can potentially release more toxin into the circulation or fail to remove the deep-seated anaerobic foci. Management focuses on neutralizing toxin (antitoxin), killing the organism (antibiotics/metronidazole), controlling spasm (sedation/muscle relaxants), and providing supportive care.	Wound debridement/excision is generally **contraindicated** in acute tetanus due to the risk of releasing more toxin; Metronidazole is preferred over Penicillin.	Human Tetanus Immunoglobulin (HTIG) should be given to neutralize unbound toxin.	Microbiology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P6, Q36: The most lethal exotoxin of, Cl. welchii is:	Hyaluronidase.	Collagenase.	Haemolysin.	Lecithinase (alpha toxin).	E. Deoxyribonuclease.	D	Lecithinase (alpha toxin).	The most lethal and important toxin of *Clostridium perfringens* (*Cl. welchii*) is the **alpha toxin (lecithinase)**. This toxin is a phospholipase C that degrades lecithin in cell membranes (including red blood cells), causing widespread hemolysis, tissue necrosis, and organ damage characteristic of gas gangrene.	Alpha toxin (lecithinase) is the key virulence factor in *Clostridium perfringens* gas gangrene.	Gas gangrene is a life-threatening surgical emergency requiring urgent debridement, antibiotics, and hyperbaric oxygen therapy.	Microbiology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P6, Q37: Which antibiotics are contraindicated in pregnant females:	Penicillins.	Cephalosporins.	Tetracyclines.	Aminoglycosides.	Lincosamines.	C	Tetracyclines.	**Tetracyclines** (e.g., Doxycycline) are contraindicated in pregnancy and children under 8 due to the risk of irreversible **staining of developing teeth** and **inhibition of bone growth**. Penicillins and Cephalosporins are generally safe.	Tetracyclines are contraindicated in pregnancy due to teeth staining and bone growth inhibition in the fetus.	Aminoglycosides (e.g., Gentamicin) are associated with fetal ototoxicity.	Pharmacology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P6, Q38: Most hands infections are caused by:	Streptococci.	Staphylococci.	E. Coli.	Anaerob.es.	Pseudomonas.	B	Staphylococci.	The majority of common skin and soft tissue infections, including most hand infections (e.g., paronychia, felon), are caused by **Staphylococcus aureus**.	The most common organism for skin and soft tissue infections is **Staphylococcus aureus**.	Hand infections frequently require surgical drainage due to the tight fascial compartments, preventing spread into deeper structures.	Infection			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P6, Q39: The hand infection which carries the highest risk of developing osteomyelitis is :	paronychia.	Intrathecal whitlow.	pulp space infection (felon).	Web space infection.	Thenar space infection.	C	pulp space infection (felon).	A **felon** is a closed-space infection of the tight fibrous compartments of the distal finger pulp. The high pressure generated by pus accumulation in this non-yielding space can compromise the blood supply to the underlying terminal phalanx, leading to **avascular necrosis** and subsequent **osteomyelitis**.	Felon (pulp space infection) high pressure risks avascular necrosis and osteomyelitis of the distal phalanx.	Osteomyelitis risk is high in any closed-space infection with pressure on bone.	Infection			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P7, Q40: Ballooning of the thenar eminence with abduction of the thumb and marked oedema of the dorsum of the hand is diagnostic of:	Subcutaneous whitlow of the thenar eminence.	Intrathecal whitlow of the thumb.	Radial bursitis.	Thenar space infection.	Dorsal subaponeurotic space infection.	D	Thenar space infection.	Infection of the **Thenar space** causes swelling/ballooning of the thenar eminence and forces the thumb into an abducted (outstretched) position due to fluid accumulation. Edema is often most visible on the dorsum of the hand due to less restrictive fascia there.	Thenar space infection classically presents with **abduction and slight flexion of the thumb** (position of rest for maximum space).	Infection in the thenar space is a deep space infection of the hand, an orthopedic emergency.	Infection			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P7, Q41: Most nosocomial infections involve the	Surgical wound.	Intravenous sites.	Respiratory tract.	Urinary tract.	Deep veins of the leg.	D	Urinary tract.	The **Urinary Tract (UTI)** is the most common site of nosocomial (hospital-acquired) infection, largely due to the frequent use of indwelling urinary catheters.	The most common hospital-acquired infection is a Catheter-Associated Urinary Tract Infection (CAUTI).	Nosocomial infections are most commonly associated with medical devices (catheters, ventilators).	Infection			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P7, Q42: Congenital dermoid cysts are characterized by the following features (except) that they:	Occur at lines of embryological fusion.	Are commonest on the face.	Are often attached to the overlying skin.	May cause hollowing of subjacent bone.	Are lined by stratified squamous epithelium.	C	Are often attached to the overlying skin.	Congenital dermoid cysts, resulting from sequestration of skin along lines of embryological fusion, are typically **not attached** or only loosely attached to the overlying skin, but they are often attached to deeper structures like periosteum and may cause remodeling (hollowing) of subjacent bone.	Congenital dermoid cysts form at fusion lines and are usually **not fixed** to the overlying skin.	They are lined by stratified squamous epithelium and contain skin appendages (hair follicles, sebaceous glands).	Dermatology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P7, Q43: Sebaceous cyst is characterized by the following except that it:	Is due to obstruction of a sebaceous gland.	Is lined by stratified squamous epithelium.	Contains a yellow pultaceous greasy material known as sebum.	May occur on the palms and soles	Is always anchored to the overlying skin at the punctum of the obstructed gland.	D	May occur on the palms and soles	So-called **Sebaceous cysts** (correctly termed Epidermal Inclusion Cysts/EIC or Pilar cysts) are lined by stratified squamous epithelium and contain keratinous debris. They cannot occur on the **palms and soles** because these areas lack sebaceous glands and hair follicles.	EIC is the correct term for a sebaceous cyst; the cyst content is keratin, not true sebum.	The characteristic dark punctum (a dimple) at the center represents the site of the blocked pore and is fixed to the overlying skin.	Dermatology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P7, Q44: A patient was operated upon under general anaesthesia at 9 a.m. Suddenly at 8 p.m. on the same day, he developed fever of 390C. The most likely diagnosis is	Acute thrombophlebitis.	Atelectasis.	Bronchopneumonia.	Wound infection.	Anastomotic leakage.	B	Atelectasis.	Postoperative fever occurring within the first **24 to 48 hours** is most commonly caused by **Atelectasis** (collapse of the lung) due to shallow breathing and retained secretions. Thrombophlebitis, wound infection, and anastomotic leak typically cause fever much later (after 3-5 days or more).	"The \5 W's\"" of postoperative fever: Wind (Atelectasis"	" day 1-2) is the most common early cause."""	Wound infection (day 5-7), Water (UTI, day 3+), Walk (DVT/PE, day 5+), Wonder drug/abscess (day 7+).			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P7, Q45: Among the ABO blood groups, agglutinogen A is absent from the cells of:	Group A.	Group B.	Group AB.	All of the above.	None of the above.	B	Group B.	Agglutinogen **A** (A antigen) is present on the red blood cells of Group A and Group AB individuals. It is **absent** from the red blood cells of Group B and Group O individuals.	Group B RBCs only carry B antigen.	Blood Group O is the universal donor (no A or B antigens); Group AB is the universal recipient (no anti-A or anti-B antibodies).	Hematology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P8, Q46: Banked blood is deficient in all of the following except:	Platelets.	White cells.	Oxygen-carrying capacity of Hb.	Potassium.	Anti-haemophilic globulin.	D	Potassium.	"Banked blood, stored for several weeks, is deficient in **Platelets** (which lose function quickly) and labile clotting factors like **Factor V and Factor VIII** (Anti-haemophilic globulin). Due to the **\storage lesion\""**"	 potassium leaks out of red blood cells into the plasma	" resulting in **high (not deficient) potassium** levels (hyperkalemia) in the transfused blood."""	"Stored blood suffers from a \storage lesion\"" including high plasma K+"			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P8, Q47: The most serious complication of blood transfusion is:	Pyrogenic reactions.	Thrombophlebitis of recipient vein.	Circulatory overloading.	Incompatibility reactions.	Viral hepatitis.	D	Incompatibility reactions.	Acute hemolytic **incompatibility reactions** (e.g., ABO mismatch) are the most dangerous, potentially life-threatening complication due to massive hemolysis, renal failure, and disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). While viral hepatitis is a serious long-term risk, acute incompatibility is the most immediate threat.	Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR) is the most immediate, life-threatening complication due to ABO mismatch.	AHTR symptoms: fever, chills, back pain, hematuria, and oliguria. Stop transfusion immediately.	Hematology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P8, Q48: Concerning potassium depletion, the incorrect statement among the following is:	Follows loss of gastrointestinal secretions.	May be produced by diuretics.	Is often associated with acidosis.	Predisposes to cardiac arrhythmias.	Produces severe muscular weakness.	C	Is often associated with acidosis.	Potassium depletion (**hypokalemia**) is more often associated with **metabolic alkalosis** (e.g., in vomiting/NG suction or with loop/thiazide diuretics). It is only associated with acidosis in cases of severe diarrhea or renal tubular acidosis.	Hypokalemia most commonly coexists with metabolic alkalosis.	The mnemonic **MURDER** is for hyperkalemia; symptoms of hypokalemia include U-waves on ECG and muscle weakness.	Fluid & Electrolytes			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P8, Q49: Acidosis may be produced by the following except:	Prolonged shock.	Hypoventilation.	Uncontrolled diabetes.	Open heart surgery.	Cirrhosis of the liver.	E	Cirrhosis of the liver.	Prolonged shock (lactic acidosis), hypoventilation (respiratory acidosis), uncontrolled diabetes (ketoacidosis), and open heart surgery (hypothermia/poor perfusion) all cause acidosis. **Cirrhosis of the liver** typically causes **metabolic alkalosis** (due to increased ammonia/renal compensation) or mixed disturbances, but rarely pure acidosis.	Cirrhosis is more often associated with **respiratory alkalosis** (hyperventilation from hepatic encephalopathy) or metabolic alkalosis.	Lactic acidosis from shock (metabolic acidosis) is a key feature in critical illness.	Acid-Base Balance			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P8, Q50: The earliest sign of hypocalcaemia is	Carpopedal spasms.	Positive Chvostek's sign.	Positive Trousseau's sign.	Tingling of fingers and circumoral region.	Defective blood coagulation,	D	Tingling of fingers and circumoral region.	The earliest and often most common subjective symptom of hypocalcemia (low calcium) is **paresthesia**, typically presenting as tingling and numbness of the fingers (acral) and the area around the mouth (circumoral). This precedes the more severe signs like tetany and seizures.	Circumoral and acral paresthesia is the earliest symptom of hypocalcemia.	Latent tetany is detected by Chvostek's sign (facial nerve twitch with tapping) or Trousseau's sign (carpal spasm with BP cuff inflation).	Fluid & Electrolytes			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P8, Q51: Which one among the following statements about cystic hygroma is untrue?	Affects infants and young children.	Occurs chiefly in the neck, axilla and groin.	Presents as a large soft fluctuating translucent swelling.	Is localized to the subcutaneous tissues.	May rupture subcutaneously with spontaneous cure.	D	Is localized to the subcutaneous tissues.	A cystic hygroma (lymphangioma) is a congenital malformation of the lymphatic system. It is **not localized** to the subcutaneous tissues; it typically extends deeply into the neck or other areas, often insinuating itself between muscles and vessels.	Cystic hygromas extend deep into tissue planes, often adhering to vital structures.	The swelling is soft, fluctuating, and **translucent** (positive transillumination).	Pediatrics			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P8, Q52: The false statement about ranula is that it:	Is a bluish cyst in the angle between the tongue and the floor of mouth.	Usually lies to one side of the middle line.	May extend into the submandibular region.	May assume an hour-glass appearance.	Is best treated by complete excision.	E	Is best treated by complete excision.	A ranula is a mucin-filled cyst from the sublingual gland. **Complete excision** is technically difficult and carries a high risk of recurrence and nerve damage; the best treatment is usually **marsupialization** (opening the cyst and stitching the edges to the mucosa to allow drainage) or excision of the sublingual gland.	Treatment of a ranula is usually Marsupialization or sublingual gland excision, not simple excision.	A plunging ranula extends through the mylohyoid muscle into the submandibular space, causing an hour-glass shape.	Head &amp; Neck Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P9, Q53: Which statement about salivary calculi is untrue?	Are commonest in the parotid gland.	May lie in the gland or its duct. Salivary stones (**sialolithiasis**) are commonest in the **submandibular gland** (about 80-90% of cases), not the parotid gland. They cause classic symptoms of pain and swelling that worsen during meals (salivary stimulation).	Sialolithiasis is overwhelmingly a disease of the submandibular gland.	Symptoms are colicky pain and swelling that increase with eating, followed by slow resolution.	Salivary Glands			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P9, Q54: Concerning pleomorphic adenoma (mixed salivary tumor), which of following statements is incorrect:	Is the commonest salivary tumor. This incomplete"" capsule is why **inadequate or superficial excision leads to a high recurrence rate**."""	Despite appearing encapsulated, pleomorphic adenomas have microscopic extensions (pseudopodia) beyond the capsule, requiring adequate surgical margin.	The tumor is usually found in the parotid gland, presenting as a firm, slow-growing, painless mass.	Salivary Glands			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P9, Q55: Among the following statements about branchial fistula, the incorrect one is that it:	Commonly results from rupture of a branchial cyst.	Usually opens externally at the lower third of the anterior border of the sternomastoid muscle.	Is often bilateral.	Discharges clear mucoid fluid.	"Requires removal of the whole track by the \step-ladder\"" operation."""	A	Commonly results from rupture of a branchial cyst.	A **Branchial fistula** (or sinus) is a congenital anomaly resulting from the **persistence** of a branchial cleft (usually the second). A branchial **cyst** is a different entity that rarely ruptures to form a fistula; cysts are typically swellings, while fistulae present as a persistent opening/drainage.	Branchial fistulae are congenital remnants, not the result of ruptured cysts.	The characteristic external opening is along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid muscle.	Head & Neck Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P9, Q56: True statements about ludwig's angina do not include that it :	Is a virulent cellulitis of the floor of the mouth. B	Usually results from infection with staphylococci.	Ludwig's Angina is a severe, rapidly spreading cellulitis of the submandibular and sublingual spaces, often caused by **streptococci** and mixed oral flora (anaerobes), not typically staphylococci. The main danger is airway compromise (suffocation) due to posterior displacement and edema of the tongue.	Ludwig's Angina is typically a polymicrobial infection, often involving **Streptococci** and anaerobes, not Staph.	The critical danger is airway compromise (suffocation) due to tongue edema/displacement, requiring urgent intubation or tracheostomy.	Infection

PE, often arising from Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), is a major cause of preventable hospital death.	Atelectasis is the most common post-op pulmonary issue; Pulmonary Embolism is the most lethal. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P10, Q58: The untrue statement about desmoid tumors of the abdominal wall is that they:	Arise from the rectus muscle or its sheath. Desmoid tumors (aggressive fibromatosis) are **benign** fibromas that are characterized by their **local invasiveness** and tendency to recur after inadequate excision, but they **do not metastasize**. They are common in multiparous women due to trauma to the rectus sheath during pregnancy.	Desmoid tumors are locally aggressive and invasive but **do not metastasize**. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P10, Q59: Concerning direct inguinal hernia, the ~incorrect statement is that it	Usually affects elderly males.	Is often bilateral. A **Direct inguinal hernia** protrudes through the weak posterior wall of the inguinal canal (**Hesselbach's triangle**) and is located **medial** to the inferior epigastric artery. An **Indirect inguinal hernia** follows the spermatic cord through the deep ring, lying **lateral** to the inferior epigastric artery.	Direct inguinal hernias are **medial** to the inferior epigastric artery (M.D.s don't L.I.E.).	Direct hernias are less prone to strangulation as they have a wide neck.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P10, Q60: True statements about paraumbilical hernia include the following except that it:	Affects females more often than males. A **Paraumbilical hernia** protrudes through a defect in the linea alba **just above or below** the umbilical scar, usually in adults (common in multiparous women). An **Umbilical hernia** (true umbilical hernia) protrudes directly through the scar itself (common in children).	A paraumbilical hernia is adjacent to the umbilicus, while an umbilical hernia is directly through the scar.	Paraumbilical hernias commonly have a multi-locular sac, which predisposes them to irreducibility and strangulation.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P10, Q61: The sac of femoral hernia protrudes through the:	Femoral ring.	Femoral canal.	Saphenous opening.	None of the above.	All of the above.	E	All of the above.	The femoral hernia sac must pass sequentially through the rigid **femoral ring**, traverse the narrow **femoral canal**, and finally exit through the **saphenous opening (fossa ovalis)** to appear as a subcutaneous mass.	The course of the femoral hernia sac includes all three anatomical spaces in succession.	Femoral hernias are highly prone to strangulation due to the narrow, rigid femoral ring.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P10, Q62: A 35-year-old multiparous female presented with a reducible right inguinal swelling which was diagnosed as a hernia. C	Indirect inguinal.	**Indirect Inguinal Hernias** are the most common hernia type in **both males and females** of all ages. While femoral hernias are more common in women than men, the indirect inguinal hernia is still more common overall, especially in this age group.	Indirect inguinal hernias are the most common type of abdominal wall hernia overall.	Femoral hernias are more common in multiparous women but are the second most common type after indirect inguinal hernia.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P11, Q63: A 70-year-old male presented with chronic constipation and abdominal distension. This is typical of large"	" longstanding hernias and explains the colon content and chronic constipation."""	A sliding hernia is one where the wall of a retroperitoneal viscus (e.g., colon, bladder) forms part of the hernial sac wall.	Sliding hernias are typically large, chronic, and more common in older men.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P11, Q64: A 25-year-old male presented with a painful tender right inguinal hernia and colicky abdominal pain. The abdominal plain X-ray showed multiple fluid levels. The correct management is by	Nasogastric suction and repeated observation.	Glycerine enema.	Taxis and truss treatment.	Urgent herniotomy.	Exploratory laparotomy.	D	Urgent herniotomy.	A painful, tender, irreducible hernia with signs of bowel obstruction (colicky pain, X-ray fluid levels) indicates a **Strangulated or Obstructed hernia**. This is a surgical emergency requiring **Urgent Herniotomy** to explore the sac, reduce or resect the contents, and repair the defect. Taxis is dangerous if strangulation is suspected.	Painful, tender, irreducible hernia is an emergency (obstruction/strangulation) and requires urgent surgery (herniotomy/hernioplasty).	Taxis (manual reduction) is contraindicated in suspected strangulation due to the risk of reducing dead/gangrenous bowel.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::General	P11, Q65: A young female presented with an extremely tender right inguinal mass which could be an inflamed inguinal lymph node or a strangulated femoral hernia. The best diagnostic measure is by:	Ultrasonography.	Abdominal X-ray.	Response to antibiotics.	Aspiration biopsy.	Operative exploration.	E	Operative exploration.	When the differential diagnosis includes an acute surgical emergency like **strangulated femoral hernia** (high mortality if delayed) and a non-surgical condition (lymphadenitis), the safest and most definitive approach is **Operative Exploration**. Delaying surgery for further tests or a trial of antibiotics is risky when strangulation is possible.	In cases of suspected strangulated hernia, the safest and fastest diagnostic and therapeutic maneuver is surgical exploration.	A strangulated femoral hernia must be ruled out immediately due to its high risk of gangrene and mortality.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P13, Q1: The commonest complicated hernia is:	Femoral	Inguinal	Lumbar	Epigastric		A	Femoral	**Femoral hernias** are the most common complicated (obstructed or strangulated) hernia type due to their consistently **narrow and rigid neck** (the femoral ring), which easily traps and compromises the blood supply of the bowel/omentum.	Femoral hernias have the highest rate of strangulation due to the narrow femoral ring.	Inguinal hernias are the most common type overall, but less commonly complicate.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P13, Q2: Subtypes of femoral hernia do not include:	Laugier hernia	Spigelian hernia	Cloquet hernia	None of the above		B	Spigelian hernia	"**Spigelian hernia** is a rare ventral hernia through the Spigelian fascia (lateral border of the rectus sheath). Laugier&#x27;s and Cloquet&#x27;s hernias are both rare anatomical variants of femoral hernias."	Spigelian hernia is a ventral hernia, not a femoral hernia variant.	A Spigelian hernia (or lateral ventral hernia) is often diagnosed by CT scan due to its inter-parietal location.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P13, Q3: Exomphalos refers to:	Congenital inguinal hernia	Femoral hernia	Congenital umbilical hernia	None of the above		C	Congenital umbilical hernia	**Exomphalos** (or **Omphalocele**) is a severe congenital defect where abdominal viscera (intestine, liver) are herniated into the base of the umbilical cord, covered only by a thin layer of peritoneum and amnion. It is a true congenital umbilical hernia.	Exomphalos (Omphalocele) is a central abdominal wall defect with viscera covered by a sac.	Gastroschisis is a lateral defect with viscera not covered by a sac.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P13, Q4: Appendecectomy may be complicated by which type of hernia:	Indirect inguinal hernia	Direct inguinal hernia	Femoral hernia	Paraumbilical hernia		B	Direct inguinal hernia	"The classic site for a hernia following a midline or paramedian incision is an **Incisional Hernia**. However, based on the provided answer key, a Direct inguinal hernia (protrudes through Hesselbach&#x27;s triangle) may occur due to generalized weakness/increased intra-abdominal pressure from the surgery/recovery, or if the original surgery was a right inguinal approach for appendicitis. Given the options, Incisional hernia is most likely but not listed. Thus, based on the key, we select Direct inguinal hernia as a common acquired form. **(Note: This is an unusual question, Incisional Hernia is the expected answer)**."	Appendectomy (especially if complicated) or any surgery that weakens the abdominal wall may predispose to incisional hernia, the most common type of post-operative hernia.	Incisional hernias are a common complication, especially with vertical incisions or infection.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P13, Q5: The following hernia is the most liable to strangulation:	Femoral hernia	Inguinal hernia	Umbilical hernia	Incisional hernia		A	Femoral hernia	As stated previously, the **Femoral hernia** has the highest risk of strangulation due to the rigid, non-yielding nature of the femoral ring which forms its neck.	The narrow, rigid neck of the femoral hernia makes it the most liable to strangulation.	Inguinal hernias are the most common but less likely to strangulate than femoral hernias.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P13, Q6: The following organ can not herniate:	Bladder	Pancreas	Caecum	All of the above		B	Pancreas	While parts of the **bladder** (in a sliding hernia) and **cecum** (also a sliding hernia) commonly herniate, the **Pancreas** is a deep retroperitoneal organ and is extremely rare (virtually impossible) to herniate through typical abdominal wall defects due to its location and strong posterior fixation.	The pancreas is strictly retroperitoneal and fixed, making its herniation highly unlikely.	Any mobile intra-abdominal or pelvic structure can herniate.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P13, Q7: As regards clinical picture of hernia:	Scar at site of hernia has no medical importance	Irreducibility predisposes to complications	Hernia is always painful	All of the above.		B	Irreducibility predisposes to complications	**Irreducibility** (cannot be pushed back in) significantly increases the risk of obstruction and strangulation, which are the main complications. Hernias are often painless (except on straining), and a scar at the site is very important as it suggests a recurrent or incisional hernia.	Irreducibility (incarceration) is the required precursor for obstruction and strangulation.	Pain that is sudden, severe, and constant in a previously reducible hernia suggests strangulation. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P13, Q8: The treatment of choice in strangulated hernia is:	R &amp; M	Urgent exploration is the rule	Reduction by taxis must be tried first	A &amp; B		D	A &amp; B	The appropriate choices for strangulated hernia are **Urgent Exploration** (which is the rule) or **Reduction and Mass (R &amp; M)** in certain centers. The provided answer key selects **D (A &amp; B)**. Urgent exploration is the definitive standard. The term R &amp; M (Reduction and Management) is ambiguous, but if it means surgical reduction and subsequent management, then D is correct. **Taxis is contraindicated** in suspected strangulation. The safest and most modern answer is Urgent exploration is the rule.&quot; We will proceed with the provided key D	 interpreting R &amp; M as **R**esuscitation and **M**anagement/Surgery	 not manual taxis.&quot;	The mainstay of treatment for a strangulated hernia is urgent surgical intervention to save the bowel (exploration and defect repair).			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P14, Q9: Ventral hernia is the:	Femoral hernia	Inguinal hernia	Internal hernia	Incisional hernia		D	Incisional hernia	A **Ventral hernia** is any hernia protruding through the anterior abdominal wall. **Incisional hernias** (occurring through a surgical scar) are the most common type of acquired ventral hernia. Umbilical and epigastric hernias are also ventral. Inguinal, femoral, and internal hernias are not classified as ventral.	Ventral hernias include incisional, umbilical, and epigastric hernias.	Incisional hernias are the most common long-term complication of abdominal surgery.	Hernia

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P14, Q11: The structure that lies between the two components of pantaloon hernia is:	Spermatic cord	Conjoint tendon	Femoral artery	Inferior epigastric artery		D	Inferior epigastric artery	A **Pantaloon (Saddlebag) hernia** is a combination of a Direct and an Indirect inguinal hernia. The two components are separated by the **Inferior Epigastric Artery**, which runs up the posterior abdominal wall and is the landmark separating the direct (medial) and indirect (lateral) hernia sites.	The Inferior Epigastric Artery divides the direct (medial) and indirect (lateral) components of a pantaloon hernia.	A pantaloon hernia is also known as a saddlebag hernia.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P14, Q12: All of the following are causes of hernia irreducibility, the commonest is:	Omental content	Adhesions	Overcrowding	Narrow neck		B	Adhesions	While all listed factors can cause irreducibility, **Adhesions** (fibrous scarring) between the contents (omentum or bowel) and the sac wall are the **most common** cause of long-term, non-acute irreducibility (incarceration) in chronic hernias. Acute irreducibility (obstruction) is often due to a narrow neck.	Adhesions between the hernia contents and the sac wall are the most common cause of non-acute irreducibility.	Incarcerated hernias are irreducible; strangulated hernias have compromised blood supply.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P14, Q13: Sliding hernia:	May contain part of bladder wall	Causes partial irreducibility	Predisposes to complications	All of the above		D	All of the above	"A Sliding Hernia (where a retroperitoneal organ forms part of the sac wall) can contain the bladder or colon, leads to partial irreducibility (due to the organ&#x27;s fixed nature), and, as a large hernia, predisposes to complications like obstruction and injury."	Sliding hernias involve retroperitoneal organs (e.g., bladder, colon) and are prone to irreducibility and injury during repair.	They are more common on the right (cecum) and left (sigmoid).	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P14, Q14: The most serious complication of hernia is:	Inflammation	Obstruction	Strangulation		C	Strangulation	**Strangulation** is the most serious complication of a hernia. It involves compromise of the blood supply to the herniated viscus, leading to ischemia, gangrene, perforation, peritonitis, and potentially death, which is a much higher risk than simple inflammation or non-strangulated obstruction.	Strangulation, with resulting bowel ischemia and death, is the most lethal complication.	Obstruction (incarceration) is the inability to pass contents; strangulation is vascular compromise.	Hernia				
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P14, Q15: As regards obturator hernia all are correct except:	More common in females	Lower limb movement induces pain	In complicated cases pain is referred to knee	All are true		D	All are true	The Obturator hernia is indeed more common in elderly, multiparous females (due to a wider pelvic brim). It causes pain with hip movement, and pain is classically referred to the medial aspect of the knee via the obturator nerve (**Howship-Romberg sign**), especially in complicated (strangulated) cases. Therefore, the statement that **all are true** is the intended correct response for an except&quot; question.&quot;	Obturator hernias (Little Old Ladies): Howship-Romberg sign is pain along the medial thigh/knee upon internal rotation.	Obturator hernias are highly prone to strangulation and difficult to diagnose pre-operatively.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Hernia	P14, Q16: The differential diagnosis of an inguinoscrotal swelling includes all of the following except:	Oblique inguinal hernia	Hydrocele of a hernial sac	Buboncele	Lipoma of the cord		C	Buboncele	A **Bubonocele** is an old term for an **Incomplete Inguinal Hernia** (specifically, one confined to the inguinal canal). Therefore, it is a type of inguinal hernia and is a core part of the differential diagnosis. The other conditions are separate entities that must be differentiated from a hernia. The question is phrased to exclude the one that is **not** a distinct differential. Given the answer choices, a Bubonocele is a type of hernia and thus not a separate differential, which makes this a tricky question. However, based on the provided answer key, C is the chosen exception.	A Bubonocele is a type of hernia (incomplete inguinal), not a completely distinct differential diagnosis like a hydrocele.	Key differential diagnoses for inguinoscrotal swellings: hernia, hydrocele, varicocele, and testicular/epididymal masses.	Hernia			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Infections	P16, Q1: As regard cl. tetani all are correct except:	Gram +ve bacilli anaerobic spore forming.	Drum stick appearance.	Have incubation period of 3-5 days.	Sensitive to penicillin.		C	Have incubation period of 3-5 days.	The incubation period of *Clostridium tetani* is highly **variable**, typically ranging from **3 days to 3 weeks** (with an average of 7-10 days), not fixed at 3-5 days. The other statements are correct.	The incubation period of tetanus is highly variable (3 days to 3 weeks); the shorter the period, the worse the prognosis.	C. tetani is sensitive to penicillin, but metronidazole is preferred for its lower seizure risk.	Microbiology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Infections	P16, Q2: Sachrolytic group of clostridia include the following except:	Cl. Welchii.	Cl. Histolyticum.	Cl. Septium.	Cl. Edematiens.		B	Cl. Histolyticum.	*Clostridium perfringens* (*Cl. welchii*), *Cl. septicum*, and *Cl. oedematiens* (now *C. novyi*) are the primary saccharolytic (sugar-fermenting) Clostridia responsible for gas gangrene. *Cl. histolyticum* is primarily **proteolytic** (protein-breaking) and is less commonly associated with gas gangrene.	Saccharolytic clostridia (welchii, septicum, oedematiens) ferment sugars, producing gas as a key feature of gas gangrene.	Proteolytic species break down proteins; saccharolytic species break down carbohydrates.	Microbiology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Infections	P16, Q3: Main pathogenic factor in tetanus is:	Local destruction.	Endotoxin causing septicemia and MOF.	Exotoxin acting on anterior horn cells and motor end plates.	Hypersensitivity reaction.		C	Exotoxin acting on anterior horn cells and motor end plates.	The pathology of tetanus is entirely due to the powerful neuro-**exotoxin**, **Tetanospasmin**, which travels retrograde to the spinal cord. It then blocks the release of inhibitory neurotransmitters (GABA and Glycine), causing unopposed motor neuron activity and resulting in spastic paralysis and spasms.	Tetanospasmin is a neurotoxin that causes spastic paralysis by blocking inhibitory neurotransmitters.	Tetanus is not an infection of the bloodstream (no septicemia); it is toxemia.	Microbiology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Infections	P16, Q4: As regard incubation period of tetanus:	Variable.	May occur up to 3weeks of wound.	The shorter the incubation period the poorer the prognosis.	All of the above.		D	All of the above.	The incubation period for tetanus is indeed variable, can be up to 3 weeks, and a shorter incubation period correlates with a larger inoculum, more rapid disease progression, and therefore a poorer prognosis.	A short incubation period (less than 7 days) indicates a severe case with a poor prognosis.	Incubation period: time from injury to the first symptom (e.g., trismus).	Microbiology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Infections	P16, Q5: Incubation period of gas gangrene is about:	1-2 days.	1-2 weeks.	Up to 1 month.	Up to 3 months.		A	1-2 days.	Gas gangrene is a rapidly progressing, life-threatening infection. The incubation period is typically very short, usually between **6 hours and 3 days** (1-2 days on average), reflecting the rapid and destructive nature of the clostridial toxins.	Gas gangrene has a rapid onset (1-3 days); a rapid onset suggests a more aggressive, lethal course.	Gas gangrene requires immediate, wide debridement of all necrotic tissue.	Infection			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Infections	P16, Q6: The earliest finding in tetanus is:	Risus sardonicus.	Trismus.	Dysphagia.	Stridor.		B	Trismus.	The earliest and most common sign of generalized tetanus is **Trismus** (lockjaw), which is stiffness of the jaw muscles. This often progresses to **Risus Sardonicus** (a fixed, sneering grin) and generalized spasms.	Trismus (lockjaw) is the first clinical sign of generalized tetanus.	Trismus is a muscle spasm (tetany) of the masseter muscles.	Microbiology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Infections	P16, Q7: The most appropriate treatment for a case of gas gangrene with multi-organ dysfunction and established muscle necrosis is:	IV antibiotics + corticosteroids.	Massive dose of antitoxin serum.	Amputation of the affected limb.	Debridement of dead muscle and limb salvage.		C	Amputation of the affected limb.	While debridement and antibiotics are the standard treatment, if the limb has **established muscle necrosis** and the patient has **multi-organ dysfunction** (a sign of severe systemic toxicity), the most appropriate life-saving measure is often **amputation** to remove the massive source of toxin and dead tissue.	Established muscle necrosis and systemic toxicity (MOF) warrant amputation to remove the source of lethal toxins.	Clostridial antitoxin is rarely used and is not a substitute for source control/debridement.	Infection			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Infections	P16, Q8: Erysipelas differs from cellulitis in:	Erysipelas is more superficial.	Erysipelas can not affect ear pinna. C	Erysipelas has sharply demarcated indurated edges.	**Erysipelas** is a superficial skin infection (dermis and upper lymphatics) that presents with **sharply demarcated, raised, indurated borders**. **Cellulitis** is a deeper infection (dermis and subcutaneous tissues) with less defined, non-raised margins. Both are caused by Group A Streptococcus (GAS).	Erysipelas is a superficial infection with raised, sharply demarcated borders.	Cellulitis is a deeper infection with flat, poorly demarcated borders. A **carbuncle** (a cluster of interconnected furuncles, or boils) is typically a deep **Staphylococcus aureus** infection. The **most common and important risk factor** for this severe skin infection is **Diabetes Mellitus (DM)**, which impairs immune function and microvascular circulation.	Diabetes Mellitus is the classic predisposing condition for carbuncles and chronic abscesses.	Carbuncles are common on the back of the neck.	Infection

B	Suffocation.	"Ludwig&#x27;s Angina is a rapidly spreading cellulitis of the floor of the mouth. Airway obstruction (suffocation) due to posterior tongue displacement/edema is the most life-threatening complication.	Urgent airway control (fiberoptic intubation or surgical airway) is the top priority.	Infection

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Infections	P17, Q14: The best site for incision of ulnar bursa abscess is:	Lateral border of hypothenar eminence.	Medial border of hypothenar eminence. Incision and drainage of an infection in the **ulnar bursa** (which surrounds the flexor tendons of the little finger and palm) should be done along the **lateral (radial) border of the hypothenar eminence** to avoid injury to the neurovascular bundles in the ulnar side.	The lateral hypothenar incision provides the safest access for drainage of the ulnar bursa.	Incisions on the palm/hand must follow the skin creases and avoid neurovascular structures.	Infection			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Infections	P17, Q15: Collar stud abscess is common with which type of hand infection:	Thenar space infection.	Subcuticular whitlow.	Ulnar bursitis.	Radial bursitis.		B	Subcuticular whitlow.	A **Collar-stud abscess** is most classically associated with a **Subcuticular Whitlow** (abscess under the skin of the finger, like a large blister). It describes an abscess with a narrow communication through the dermis, joining a superficial compartment to a deep compartment, resembling the shape of a collar stud (or button). Based on the provided answer key, **Subcuticular whitlow** is the correct answer. The other classic presentation is **web space infection**.	A collar-stud abscess is characterized by a superficial component connected by a narrow tract to a deep component.	Web space infection is another common cause of a collar-stud abscess.	Infection

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P20, Q1: The most useful investigation for a breast with a prosthesis is:	Mammography.	US.	FNABC.	MRI.	Hormonal receptors.	D	MRI.	Magnetic Resonance Imaging (**MRI**) is the most sensitive and specific imaging modality for detecting breast cancer, especially in breasts with **prostheses**, where mammography sensitivity is reduced due to the implant material obscuring the tissue. MRI is also used to assess for implant rupture.	MRI is superior for breast imaging with implants or in high-risk screening, and for assessing implant integrity.	Mammography is limited by the density of the implant material.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P20, Q2: Triple assessment include the following except:	Clinical evaluation.	Imaging.	Laboratory investigations.	Cytology.		C	Laboratory investigations.	The **Triple Assessment** for a breast lump comprises: 1) **Clinical Examination**, 2) **Imaging** (Mammography and/or Ultrasound), and 3) **Pathological Assessment** (Fine-Needle Aspiration Cytology/FNAC or Core Biopsy). **Laboratory Investigations** (e.g., blood tests) are not part of the core triple assessment.	Triple assessment: Clinical exam, Imaging, and Pathology (FNAC/Biopsy).	A diagnosis of malignancy requires concordance across all three components.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P20, Q3: Etiology of breast abscess include:	Mostly in lactating women.	Due to Staph from baby mouth.	Bad hygiene.	All of the above.		D	All of the above.	"Breast abscesses are most common in **lactating women** (puerperal) due to milk stagnation and cracked nipples. The infection is typically caused by **Staphylococcus aureus**, often originating from the infant&#x27;s mouth or the mother&#x27;s skin. Poor hygiene and underlying conditions contribute to the risk."	Breast abscesses are most common in lactating women due to *Staphylococcus aureus* entering via cracked nipples.	Initial treatment for mastitis is antibiotics, but an established abscess requires drainage (aspiration or incision).	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P20, Q4: Signs of pus formation are the following except:	Hectic fever.	Edema of overlying skin.	No response to medical treatment for 48 hours.	Fluctuation occurs early in breast abscess.		D	Fluctuation occurs early in breast abscess.	**Fluctuation** (the feeling of a fluid-filled cavity) is a definitive sign of abscess formation, but in the tight, tense tissue of the breast, it often occurs **late** in the course of the disease, or may not be easily detectable until the abscess is very large or superficial. Hectic fever, edema, and failure to improve with antibiotics are all indicators of pus formation.	Fluctuation is a late sign in breast abscess due to the dense glandular tissue.	The diagnosis of a breast abscess is confirmed by ultrasound, which can also guide aspiration.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P20, Q5: Best treatment of chronic breast abscess is:	Prolonged antibiotic courses.	Repeated aspiration.	Incision and drainage.	Excision of the whole abscess.		D	Excision of the whole abscess.	For a **chronic breast abscess** (often non-puerperal, potentially related to periductal mastitis), simple incision and drainage or repeated aspiration often leads to recurrence or a persistent sinus. The best treatment is **Excision of the whole abscess** and associated involved ducts to prevent recurrence and allow for definitive pathology examination to rule out malignancy.	Chronic or recurrent breast abscesses require definitive treatment by **excision** to remove the diseased tissue/ducts and rule out cancer.	Acute breast abscesses are usually treated by needle aspiration or incision and drainage.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P20, Q6: Clinical findings of fibroadenosis include:	Cyclic pain.	Cyclic swelling.	Cyclic discharge.	All of the above.		D	All of the above.	**Fibroadenosis** (or Fibrocystic Changes) is a common benign breast condition characterized by symptoms that fluctuate with the menstrual cycle. **Cyclic pain (mastalgia)** and **swelling/lumpiness** are classic, and a **cyclic discharge** (usually bilateral, watery, or green/brown) may also occur.	Fibroadenosis symptoms (pain, swelling, tenderness) are classic for varying with the **menstrual cycle**.	This condition is a benign change and not a true disease, but it must be differentiated from cancer.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P20, Q7: Cyst of Blood Good is:	Retention cyst of duct papilloma.	Precancerous lesion of breast.	Giant fibroadenoma. A **Cyst of Blood Good** is an older term for a **haemorrhagic cyst** of the breast containing altered (old) blood. It is a variant of fibrocystic change. This is a descriptive term and not a specific entity in modern classification.	The term Cyst of Blood Good is an older synonym for a hemorrhagic or apoplectic breast cyst.	Ultrasound is used to confirm the cystic nature and monitor the lesion.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P21, Q8: Treatment of fibroadenosis all true except:	Psychotherapy has a main role.	Primrose improves the condition.	Initial treatment should include antiestrogen.	Surgery is reserved to complicated cases.		C	Initial treatment should include antiestrogen.	Initial treatment for fibroadenosis is typically **conservative** (reassurance, supportive bra, NSAIDs, evening primrose oil/Gamma-linolenic acid). **Antiestrogens** (like Tamoxifen or Danazol) are reserved for **severe, refractory** cases due to their side effects, not as initial treatment. Surgery is rare, typically only for a persistent, dominant cyst.	Initial treatment is conservative (reassurance, primrose oil, analgesics); anti-estrogens are reserved for severe, refractory cases.	The condition is usually due to an exaggerated response to normal hormonal fluctuations.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	"P21,Q9: The commonest route of infection of breast abscess is:"""	Along blood vessels	Retrograde infection along lymphatic veseels	Along natural passages (nipple)	Along artificial passages like fissures or cracks of nipple and areola	Local extension from infection of the chest wall muscles or ribs	C	Along natural passages (nipple)	Infection in puerperal (lactational) mastitis and abscess most commonly starts by bacteria (e.g., *S. aureus*) entering through **fissures or cracks in the nipple and areola**[cite: 728], which are essentially artificial passages connecting to the natural duct system. Among the given options, **Along natural passages (nipple)** is the closest description of the main entry point via the ducts.	Infection starts when bacteria from the baby's mouth enter the ducts through cracks in the nipple/areola.	*Staphylococcus aureus* is the causative organism.

"**Mondor&#x27;s disease** is a rare, self-limiting condition involving **superficial thrombophlebitis** (inflammation and clotting) of the thoracoepigastric or lateral thoracic veins, which can affect the breast, chest wall, or axilla, presenting as a tender cord-like structure."	"Mondor&#x27;s disease is superficial thrombophlebitis of the chest wall/breast veins, presenting as a tender, palpable cord."
