Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P21, Q11: Bleeding on zonal pressure is highly suggestive of:	Breast abscess.	Duct papilloma.	Cancer breast.	Cystosarcoma phylloides.		B	Duct papilloma.	"**Bleeding from the nipple on zonal pressure** (compressing the breast radially at the site corresponding to the affected duct) is a classic sign of an **Intraductal Papilloma**. This benign tumor causes bleeding due to trauma from the duct&#x27;s wall compressing the small, friable, vascular growth. Cancer is a less common cause of bloody discharge."	Bloody nipple discharge from a single duct is highly characteristic of an **intraductal papilloma**.	The main differential for bloody nipple discharge is intraductal papilloma vs. ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS).	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P21, Q12: Best treatment for duct papilloma is:	Follow up.	Local excision.	Local excision with safety margin.	Chemotherapy and/or radiation.		C	Local excision with safety margin.	An Intraductal Papilloma is a benign, but sometimes pre-malignant, lesion. The best treatment is **local excision of the involved duct system (microdochectomy)**, including a surrounding margin of breast tissue, to ensure complete removal and provide tissue for definitive histology to rule out an associated carcinoma in situ.	Microdochectomy (excision of the duct/papilloma complex) is the treatment of choice to remove the lesion and allow for definitive diagnosis.	Simple local excision without duct removal carries a higher recurrence risk.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P21, Q13: The following is treatment of choice in pericanalicular fibroadenoma:	Irradiation.	Enucleation.	Removal en block.	Simple mastectomy.		B	Enucleation.	A **Fibroadenoma** (pericanalicular or intracanalicular) is a benign, well-circumscribed, and highly mobile tumor (breast mouse). Because it is completely encapsulated, the treatment of choice is surgical **enucleation** (shelling out the tumor from its capsule), which is a simple, curative procedure.	Fibroadenomas (benign) are removed by **enucleation** (shelling out) due to their clear capsule.	A key differential, Cystosarcoma Phyllodes, requires wider excision/removal *en bloc*.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P21, Q14: Probe test can differentiate cystosarcoma phylloides from:	Fibroadenosis.	Breast mouse.	Cancer breast.	Chronic breast abscess.		C	Cancer breast.	The **Probe Test** (or sliding sign) helps differentiate a mobile, non-invasive lesion (like a fibroadenoma or a Cystosarcoma Phyllodes) from an **Infiltrating Cancer** of the breast. A cancer is typically fixed to the surrounding tissue, while the non-invasive tumor can often be \probed\&quot; or pushed away from the underlying fascia/muscle.&quot;	The Probe Test (or sliding sign) assesses for tumor **fixity** to underlying pectoralis muscle, a sign of advanced cancer.	A mobile tumor is less likely to be an infiltrating carcinoma.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P22, Q15: Risk factors of cancer breast include all the following except:	Mutation in suppressor genes (BRCA I, II).	Early menarche.	Carcinoma in situ.	Prolonged lactation.	White races.	D	Prolonged lactation.	**Prolonged lactation** is thought to be **protective** against breast cancer, not a risk factor. All the others are recognized risk factors: BRCA mutations (hereditary risk), early menarche (longer estrogen exposure), carcinoma in situ (pre-malignant lesion), and Caucasian race.	Prolonged lactation is a **protective** factor against breast cancer.	Other risk factors include late menopause, nulliparity, and advanced age.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P22, Q16: Increased occurrence of cancer breast in upper lateral quadrant is due to:	Increased estrogen receptors.	Increased breast mass.	Both of the above.	None of the above.		C	Both of the above.	The majority of breast tissue, and therefore the greatest **breast mass**, is concentrated in the **Upper Outer Quadrant (UOQ)**, which extends into the axilla (the **Tail of Spence**). Cancers are generally proportional to the volume of tissue at risk, although some texts also cite high local hormone activity. Given the options, **Both of the above** is the intended answer.	The upper outer quadrant has the largest volume of glandular tissue, making it the most common site for breast cancer.	Tumors in the UOQ also drain primarily to the axillary nodes, the main route of metastasis.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	"P22, Q17: Peu d&#x27;orange is due to:"	Skin metastasis.	Lymphatic obstruction.	Thrombophlebitis.	Any of the above.		B	Lymphatic obstruction.	"**Peau d&#x27;orange** (orange peel skin) is a classic sign of advanced breast cancer. It is caused by **obstruction of the dermal lymphatics** by tumor cells or inflammation, leading to lymphatic fluid accumulation and pitting edema of the skin, where the hair follicles remain tethered, creating the characteristic dimpled appearance."	"Peau d&#x27;orange results from lymphatic obstruction in the dermis, often by advanced cancer."	It is an ominous clinical sign indicating locally advanced, inflammatory-type breast cancer.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P22, Q18: Cancer en cuirasse is:	Skin nodule.	Lymphedema of breast skin.	Both.	None of the above.		C	Both.	**Cancer en cuirasse** is an advanced, rare form of breast cancer that infiltrates the skin in a hard, plate-like fashion (**cuirasse** means breastplate). It is characterized by widespread **skin nodules** and severe **brawny lymphedema** of the skin, making it rigid and indurated.	Cancer en cuirasse is a clinical description of diffuse, hardening, plate-like skin infiltration by breast cancer.	It is a sign of poor prognosis and local recurrence.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P22, Q19: The cause in skin dimpling in any fibrotic condition of the breast is the affection of:	Milk duct..	Milk acini.	"Cooper&#x27;s ligament."	All of the above.		C	"Cooper&#x27;s ligament."	"**Skin dimpling** is caused by shortening or retraction of the suspensory **Cooper&#x27;s ligaments** (fibrous septa that anchor the breast skin to the fascia). Fibrosis (either benign or due to cancer) pulls on these ligaments, causing the overlying skin to retract and dimple."	"Skin dimpling/retraction is caused by fibrosis/tumor pulling on the suspensory Cooper&#x27;s ligaments."	The dimple is best visualized with the patient raising her arms or pressing her hands on her hips.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P22, Q20: Contraindications to conservative surgery include:	Large tumor.	"Paget&#x27;s disease of nipple."	Previous irradiation.	Distant metastasis.	All of the above.	E	All of the above.	"**Conservative Breast Surgery (Lumpectomy/Quadrantectomy)** followed by radiation is contraindicated if the tumor is too large for a cosmetic result (large tumor), if the patient has a multifocal/multicentric disease (Paget&#x27;s is a form of DCIS), if prior radiation makes the area too fragile (Previous irradiation), or if the patient has disseminated, systemic disease (Distant metastasis)."	Absolute contraindications for breast conservation include prior radiation, diffuse malignant microcalcifications, or positive surgical margins.	Distant metastasis indicates systemic, palliative treatment, making local conservative surgery inappropriate.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P23, Q21: The following is contraindication to conservative breast surgery:	Palpable mobile axillary LNs	Tumor 3 cm	Mastitis carcinomatosis	Patient aged 50 years		C	Mastitis carcinomatosis	**Mastitis Carcinomatosis** (or Inflammatory Breast Cancer, IBC) is a highly aggressive cancer presenting with signs of inflammation. It is generally a **contraindication** to conservative surgery and is typically treated with neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by mastectomy and radiation. A mobile LN or a 3 cm tumor alone is not a contraindication.	Inflammatory Breast Cancer (Mastitis Carcinomatosis) is a contraindication to conservative surgery and requires systemic therapy (chemotherapy) first.	"IBC is characterized by rapid onset, edema, erythema (Peau d&#x27;orange), and tumor emboli in dermal lymphatics."	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P23, Q22: All the following are recognized side effects of radiotherapy except:	Local burn.	Pulmonary fibrosis.	End arteritis.	None of the above.		D	None of the above.	Radiotherapy (RT) to the breast is associated with local skin reaction (local burn), damage to the microvasculature (end arteritis), and long-term lung damage (radiation pneumonitis/pulmonary fibrosis) in the underlying area. Therefore, **None of the above** is the correct answer, meaning all listed conditions **are** recognized side effects.	Common side effects of breast RT include dermatitis, pneumonitis/fibrosis, and rib fracture.	RT is a standard component of breast-conserving therapy (BCT).	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P23, Q23: Adjuvant chemotherapy is indicated in:	+ve LN biopsy.	-ve hormonal receptors.	Poor prognosis of the case.	All of the above.		D	All of the above.	**Adjuvant chemotherapy** (given after local treatment) is indicated for patients with a high risk of systemic recurrence. This includes positive lymph nodes (LN), tumors that are negative for hormone receptors (ER-/PR-) or HER2- (Triple-Negative, as these are often aggressive), or other features indicating a **poor prognosis** (large size, high grade, young age).	Adjuvant chemotherapy is used for high-risk systemic disease (e.g., node-positive, triple-negative, large tumor size).	Hormone receptor-positive patients also benefit from adjuvant endocrine therapy (e.g., Tamoxifen).	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P23, Q24: Chemotherapy is the primary palliative treatment in the following conditions:	Visceral metastasis.	Advanced case in premenopausal woman.	-ve hormonal receptors with distant metastasis.	All of the above.		D	All of the above.	**Chemotherapy** is the primary systemic treatment for patients with advanced or metastatic breast cancer, particularly when the disease is aggressive. It is the preferred palliative treatment for highly proliferative tumors (e.g., visceral metastasis), in premenopausal women (where ovarian suppression is less effective alone), and in tumors that are hormone receptor-negative (where Tamoxifen/Aromatase inhibitors are ineffective).	Chemotherapy is the first-line palliative systemic therapy for aggressive, hormone receptor-negative, or visceral metastatic breast cancer.	Endocrine therapy (Tamoxifen) is preferred for hormone receptor-positive patients.	Breast

A	Is more common than ductal carcinoma.	**Infiltrating Ductal Carcinoma (IDC)** is by far the **most common** histological type of breast cancer (about 70-80%). **Infiltrating Lobular Carcinoma (ILC)** is the second most common (about 10-15%). Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P23, Q28: Indian file arrangement is a histological picture of the following breast tumor:	Paget disease	Lobular carcinoma in situ	Ductal carcinoma in situ	Infiltrating lobular carcinoma		D	Infiltrating lobular carcinoma	**Infiltrating Lobular Carcinoma (ILC)** is histologically characterized by small, uniform tumor cells that invade the breast stroma in a single-file line (the **\Indian file\&quot;** pattern). This pattern is due to the loss of E-cadherin expression	 which is a hallmark of ILC.&quot;	"The classic histological feature of Infiltrating Lobular Carcinoma is the \Indian file\"" linear arrangement of cells."""	This pattern of invasion makes ILC difficult to detect by mammography.

Cytotoxic chemotherapy (CMF) is effective but may be reserved for higher-risk cases. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P24, Q30: Clinical features of breast cancer which are associated with a particularly poor prognosis include:	Edema of the skin of the breast	Skin ulceration	Lateral arm edema	Dermal lymphatic invasion	All of the above	E	All of the above	All listed clinical features indicate a locally advanced or systemic stage of breast cancer, which are signs of a **poor prognosis**. Edema of the skin (**Peau d'orange**), **ulceration**, **arm edema** (due to axillary involvement/lymphatic block), and **dermal lymphatic invasion** (a histological feature of inflammatory cancer) all reflect aggressive disease.	Signs of locally advanced breast cancer (T4 stage: edema, ulceration, fixity) are associated with a very poor prognosis.	Arm edema suggests significant axillary lymph node involvement.				
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P24, Q31: A 21-year-old woman presents with an asymptomatic breast mass. Which of the following statement(s) is/are true concerning her diagnosis and treatment? US can differentiate a benign solid lesion (like a fibroadenoma) from a simple cyst. The mass should **not always be excised** if benign features are confirmed."	In women under 30, ultrasound is the preferred imaging modality due to dense breast tissue.	The most common breast mass in this age group is a benign fibroadenoma or a cyst.	Breast

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P24, Q33: The acronym QUART stands for:	Quadrantectomy and radiotherapy	Quadrentectomy axillary dissection and radiotherapy	Quadrant resection and chemotherapy	None of the above		B	Quadrentectomy axillary dissection and radiotherapy	**QUART** stands for **QU**adrantectomy, **A**xillary dissection, and **R**adiotherapy, a procedure used in the breast-conserving management of certain breast cancers. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P24, Q34: When stage I breast cancer is treated by partial mastectomy and dissection, further therapy should include:	Nothing.	Chemotherapy.	Antioestrogen agents.	Radiation of the affected breast.	Oophorectomy if premenopausal.	D	Radiation of the affected breast.	For most patients who undergo **partial mastectomy (lumpectomy)** as part of Breast Conserving Therapy (BCT), **Radiation of the affected breast** is an essential part of the treatment to reduce the risk of local recurrence and is the key component often missed in this context. Systemic therapy (chemotherapy/endocrine) depends on tumor features (ER/PR/HER2 status, grade, nodes).	Radiation therapy to the residual breast is mandatory after lumpectomy for invasive cancer to reduce local recurrence.	Radiation therapy in BCT reduces the local recurrence risk by approximately two-thirds.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P25, Q35: Massive swellings of the breast include all the following except:	Cystosarcoma phylloides	Atrophic scirrhous carcinoma.	Diffuse hypertrophy.	Giant fibroadenoma.		B	Atrophic scirrhous carcinoma.	**Atrophic Scirrhous Carcinoma** is a common type of breast cancer that causes significant fibrosis, resulting in a **small, hard, retracted** lump in a postmenopausal woman, leading to retraction and shrinkage of the breast (atrophy). It does **not** cause a massive swelling. The others (Cystosarcoma, Hypertrophy, Giant Fibroadenoma) are all causes of massive breast swelling.	Atrophic scirrhous carcinoma causes breast **shrinkage and retraction**, not massive swelling.	Cystosarcoma Phyllodes is a large, rapidly growing mass that can become massive.	Breast			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P25, Q36: Tamoxifen use in breast cancer causes all Except:	Decreases recurrence in affected breast.	Decreases incidence in contralateral breast.	Increase incidence of endometrial cancer.	Increased incidence of myocardial infarction.		D	Increased incidence of myocardial infarction.	**Tamoxifen** is a Selective Estrogen Receptor Modulator (SERM). It acts as an anti-estrogen in the breast, decreasing recurrence and contralateral cancer incidence. It acts as a weak estrogen agonist in the uterus (**increasing endometrial cancer risk**) and bone, and it **reduces** (not increases) the incidence of myocardial infarction by favorably affecting the lipid profile.	Tamoxifen is a SERM that acts as an **estrogen agonist in the bone and endometrium** (risk of cancer) and an **antagonist in the breast**.	Tamoxifen is associated with a **reduced risk** of cardiovascular events/MI.	Pharmacology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P25, Q37: Incurable breast cancer in premenopausal females may be treated by the following except:	Local mastectomy	Radiotherapy	Estrogen administration	Chemotherapy	Tamoxifen		C	Estrogen administration	For incurable (metastatic) breast cancer in premenopausal women, the goal is palliation using systemic treatments. This includes hormone therapy (Tamoxifen, Oophorectomy/Ovarian Suppression) and chemotherapy. **Estrogen administration** is a strong **contraindication** as it is trophic to the tumor cells and will worsen the disease.	Estrogen administration is contraindicated in breast cancer, as the tumors are often estrogen-dependent and it will fuel growth.	The treatment goal for incurable cancer is palliative, focusing on quality of life and controlling disease spread.

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Breast	P25, Q41: Causes of gynecomastia:	Liver cell failure.	Digitalis.	Spironolactone.	Orchiectomy.	All of the above	E	All of the above	**Gynecomastia** (benign enlargement of male breast tissue) can be caused by increased estrogen or decreased testosterone. Causes include: **Liver cell failure** (impaired estrogen metabolism), **Digitalis** (estrogen-like effect), **Spironolactone** (anti-androgen/estrogen agonist), and **Orchiectomy** (removal of testis, leading to low testosterone).	Gynecomastia causes are categorized as increased estrogen, decreased androgen, or medications (e.g., Spironolactone, Digitalis).	The key mechanism is an increased estrogen-to-androgen ratio.	Endocrinology

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P29, Q3: Empty scrotum may be due to:	Testicular agenesis.	Fetal testicular torsion.	Arrested testis.	Retractile testis.	All of the above	E	All of the above	An apparently empty scrotum can be due to a genuine absence of the testis (**agenesis**), a testis that was present but underwent atrophy after a **fetal torsion**, a testis that failed to descend (**arrested/undescended testis**), or a testis that is temporarily pulled up out of the scrotum by the cremasteric reflex (**retractile testis**). Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P29, Q5: All of the following are causes of unilateral testicular arrest except:	Testicular dysgenesis.	Low maternal HCG.	Malformed inguinal canal. Testicular arrest (cryptorchidism) is often multifactorial. **Low maternal HCG** (Human Chorionic Gonadotropin) during the period of testicular descent is a hormonal factor that would typically affect **both** testes, making it a less likely cause of a **unilateral** defect. The other options are physical/anatomic defects that can easily be unilateral.	Low maternal HCG is a systemic/hormonal factor, more likely to cause **bilateral** cryptorchidism or affect the timing of descent.	A short spermatic cord/testicular artery is a physical barrier to descent.	Urology

[cite_start]The reason is not entirely clear, but it is thought to be related to the later descent of the right testis and its longer path in the abdomen.	Cryptorchidism is more common on the right side.	Bilateral cryptorchidism occurs in about 10-20% of cases[cite: 1125].	Urology				
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P29, Q7: Risk factors of testicular arrest include:	+ve family history.	Down syndrome.	Teratogenic drugs.	Chronic maternal illness.	All of the above. Testicular arrest (cryptorchidism) is associated with all of the factors listed, suggesting a complex etiology involving genetic, chromosomal, and environmental factors. **Family history** and **chromosomal anomalies** (Down syndrome) are known, as are **teratogenic drugs** and **maternal health** issues.	Cryptorchidism has a multifactorial etiology: genetic, hormonal, and anatomic defects.	Descent of the testes is a complex process dependent on both hormonal (testosterone, AMH) and mechanical factors.	Urology

The majority of undescended testes are palpable, with the **inguinal canal** (either within the canal or at the external ring) being the most common site of arrest. The abdomen and pelvis are less common sites.	The inguinal canal is the most common site for an arrested testis.	Approximately 80% of undescended testes are palpable.	Urology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P30, Q10: The following are complications of arrested testis:	Torsion.	Trauma.	Tumor.	All of the above.		D	All of the above.	An arrested (undescended) testis is at an increased risk of all listed complications. The abnormal fixation predisposes it to **Torsion**, its location makes it susceptible to **Trauma**, and it has a dramatically increased risk of developing a **Testicular Tumor** (seminoma is most common).	Undescended testes have a higher risk of sterility, hernia, trauma, and malignant transformation.	Malignancy risk is highest for intra-abdominal testes.	Urology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P30, Q11: The most diagnostic investigation in arrested testis is:	US.	CT.	Laparoscopy.	Doppler.		C	Laparoscopy.	For a **non-palpable** undescended testis, **Laparoscopy** is considered the **most diagnostic** (and often therapeutic) procedure. It is the gold standard because it can definitively identify the testis (or its remnants) if it is intra-abdominal and determine the surgical plan. Ultrasound (US) is good for palpable testes but poor for intra-abdominal ones.	Laparoscopy is the gold standard for diagnosis and management of a non-palpable testis.	Ultrasound is usually performed first to try and locate the testis in the inguinal canal.	Urology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P30, Q12: Testis not found by laparoscopy may be due to:	Testicular agenesis.	Fetal testicular torsion.	Maldescended testis.	All of the above.		D	All of the above.	If a testis is non-palpable and not found by laparoscopy, the patient has an absence of a viable testis, which could be due to a true failure to form (**agenesis**) or a late-stage atrophy following a perinatal or **fetal torsion** (vanishing testis syndrome). A **maldescended testis** would have been found, but this term can be broadly applied to the cause of the absence. Given the options, all are possible explanations for a non-found testis/remnant.	The non-palpable, non-found testis is often due to agenesis or a vanished (atrophied) testis from fetal torsion.	Laparoscopy will show no vas deferens/vessels (agenesis) or blind-ending vessels (vanished testis).	Urology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P30, Q13: Best time for orchiopexy is:	At 1st day of life.	6-15 months.	At 5 years.	At puberty.		B	6-15 months.	To maximize the potential for germ cell survival and to reduce the risk of future malignancy, the current consensus is that surgical correction (**Orchiopexy**) should be performed between **6 and 15 months** of age. Earlier is not better; later dramatically decreases germ cell viability.	Orchiopexy should be performed between 6 and 15 months to preserve fertility potential and simplify the procedure.	Delaying surgery beyond 18 months significantly compromises the potential for fertility.	Urology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P30, Q14: Best treatment of retractile testis is:	Reassurance.	Medical treatment.	Orchiopexy.	Orchidectomy.		A	Reassurance.	A **Retractile testis** is a normal testicle that is pulled up out of the scrotum by a hyperactive cremasteric reflex. It is **not** an undescended testis. No surgery or medical treatment is needed; the patient/parents only require **reassurance** that it is normal and will usually descend fully at puberty.	A retractile testis is a normal variant and requires only reassurance and observation; no surgery is needed.	Retractile testes can be easily manipulated back into the scrotum and will remain there.	Urology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P31, Q15: Concerning undescended testis, the following statements are true except that it:	Affects about 1% of all males	Is commoner on the right than on the left side	Is bilateral in about 20% of cases	May be intra-abdominal or extra-abdominal	Is rarely associated with inguinal hernia	E	Is rarely associated with inguinal hernia	An undescended testis is almost **always associated with a patent processus vaginalis** (PPV), which is the anatomical basis for an **indirect inguinal hernia**. Therefore, it is **commonly** associated with an indirect inguinal hernia, making this statement the incorrect one.	Undescended testis is commonly associated with an **indirect inguinal hernia** due to a patent processus vaginalis.	A hernia sac is found in 90% of cases of palpable undescended testes.	Urology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P31, Q16: The following statements about ectopic testis are true except that it:	Is probably due to rupture of the scrotal tail of the gubernaculum. An **Ectopic Testis** is one that has completed its descent through the inguinal canal but then migrated to an abnormal position (e.g., superficial inguinal pouch, pubic region). Since its blood supply and nerve supply are intact, the testis usually **develops normally**. The other statements about its location and cord length are correct.	An ectopic testis has usually developed normally because the cord length is normal and blood supply is preserved.	The most common ectopic site is the superficial inguinal pouch.	Urology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P31, Q17: Best treatment for testicular torsion is:	Resuscitation.	Early operation to untwist the testis.	Orchiopexy for other testis.	All of the above in sequence.		D	All of the above in sequence.	The treatment for acute testicular torsion requires a sequential and urgent approach: **Resuscitation** and pain control, **Early Operation** (within 6 hours if possible) to untwist the affected testis and assess viability, and then a prophylactic **Orchiopexy for the contralateral testis** (to prevent torsion in the future, as the bell-clapper anomaly is often bilateral).	The management sequence for torsion is emergent exploration/detorsion of the affected testis, followed by prophylactic fixation (orchiopexy) of both testes.	Torsion is a surgical emergency: time is tissue.	Urology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P31, Q18: While lifting a heavy weight, an adolescent male felt sudden severe pain in the testis, groin and lower abdomen associated with vomiting, sweating and collapse. Lifting a heavy weight is a potential trigger, and the pain is due to ischemia.	Sudden, severe, unremitting unilateral testicular pain with systemic symptoms (vomiting) in an adolescent is torsion until proven otherwise.	An inflamed epididymo-orchitis is usually subacute and associated with fever and urinary symptoms.	Urology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P31, Q19: Regarding choriocarcinoma all are correct except:	It is a subtype of teratoma.	Rarely gives lung metastasis.	Secretes HCG in large amounts. It is a subtype of NSGCT and secretes very high levels of **Human Chorionic Gonadotropin (HCG)**.	Choriocarcinoma is a highly malignant testicular tumor that characteristically metastasizes **early and frequently** to the lungs and brain.	HCG is the key tumor marker for Choriocarcinoma. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P31, Q21: The 1st LN station draining testis is:	Inguinal.	Internal iliac.	Paraaortic.	Supraclavicular.		C	Paraaortic.	Because the testis descends from the abdomen, its lymphatic drainage follows the testicular artery back to the **Paraaortic (or Retroperitoneal)** lymph nodes, which are the first echelon of nodes. Only when the tunica vaginalis or scrotum is involved does it drain to the inguinal nodes.	Testicular lymphatics drain primarily to the retroperitoneal (paraaortic/paracaval) lymph nodes. The most common presentation of testicular cancer is a **painless, hard, fixed mass** in the testicle, often discovered accidentally by the patient. Pain is rare, and systemic symptoms (bone pain, hemoptysis from lung mets) are late signs.	The classic presentation of testicular cancer is a **painless** testicular mass (hard and fixed). Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P32, Q23: Radiosensitive testicular neoplasm is:	Seminoma.	Teratoma.	None of the above.	All of the above.		A	Seminoma.	**Seminoma** (a Germ Cell Tumor) is highly **radiosensitive** and is traditionally treated with orchiectomy followed by adjuvant radiation for localized disease (though chemotherapy is becoming more common). **Non-Seminomatous Germ Cell Tumors (NSGCTs)**, including Teratoma, are generally considered **radioresistant**.	Seminoma is a highly radiosensitive testicular tumor.	NSGCTs (Teratoma, Embryonal Carcinoma, Choriocarcinoma) are radioresistant.	Urology

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P33, Q33: The vaginal hydrocele is characterized by all of the following except:	Fluctuation is positive	Eversion and excision of the tunica is the main line of treatment	The swelling is usually found above the neck of the scrotum	Aspiration is followed by recurrence		C	The swelling is usually found above the neck of the scrotum	A **Vaginal Hydrocele** (the common primary type) is a collection of fluid within the tunica vaginalis, which surrounds the testicle. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Testis	P33, Q34: Size fluctuation is a character of:	Infantile hydrocele.	Congenital hydrocele.	Both A and B.	None of the above.		B	Congenital hydrocele.	**Size fluctuation** (swelling being larger at night or decreasing in size when the child lies down) is the hallmark of a **Congenital Hydrocele**. This is due to a **patent processus vaginalis** (PPV) allowing peritoneal fluid to flow freely from the abdominal cavity into the tunica vaginalis. D	Encysted hydrocele of the cord.	The **Traction Test** is a physical maneuver used to diagnose an **Encysted Hydrocele of the Cord**. The cord cyst is usually oval and freely mobile along the cord; applying traction to the testis will **fix** the cyst, confirming its attachment to the cord structure.	The Traction Test fixes the swelling and is diagnostic of an encysted hydrocele of the cord.	An encysted hydrocele of the cord is a localized collection of fluid within the remnant of the processus vaginalis.	Urology

Spermatoceles are filled with fluid (and sperm) and are **translucent** on transillumination, unlike solid tumors.	It is often fixed to the upper pole of the testis/epididymis.	Urology

The **Positive Traction Test** is the key feature that distinguishes an **Encysted Hydrocele of the Cord** from other scrotal or inguinal swellings. The ability to get above the swelling is seen in all scrotal swellings (hernia excluded), and fluctuation/translucency are common to all fluid-filled cysts (hydrocele/spermatocele). Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P37, Q5: The action of heparin in arterial embolism is to:	Deal with cardiac problem	Prevent propagation of thrombosis	Prevent further embolization	All of the above		D	All of the above	In the context of arterial embolism, intravenous **Heparin** has several critical actions: it prevents local **propagation of the thrombus** distal to the embolus, helps to stabilize the heart rhythm (dealing with the **cardiac problem** like Atrial Fibrillation, the source of the embolus), and when started immediately, reduces the risk of a **further embolization**. Thus, **All of the above** is the most complete answer.	Heparin's primary local role is to prevent propagation of the distal clot, but its systemic role includes reducing the risk of further emboli from the source.	Heparin is not a clot dissolver; it is an anticoagulant.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P37, Q6: In a traumatized patient showing signs of fracture and acute ischemia:	Urgent repair of the affected vessel	Urgent ligation of the affected vessels	Reduction of the fracture and waiting for return of pulse is the 1st step	None of the above		C	Reduction of the fracture and waiting for return of pulse is the 1st step	In a combined fracture and vascular injury (e.g., knee dislocation), the **first step** is to perform **closed reduction of the fracture/dislocation** and reassess the pulses. If the pulse returns, the arterial injury may have been due to spasm or kinking, which is relieved. If the pulse remains absent, an urgent vascular intervention is needed.	Reduction of a fractured or dislocated limb should always precede definitive vascular repair to re-establish blood flow if possible.	Failure to restore the pulse after reduction indicates a definite arterial injury requiring surgical exploration.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P37, Q7: Arterial embolism may result from:	Atherosclerosis	Bone fracture	Parasites	All of the above		D	All of the above	An arterial embolus is any material transported by the bloodstream that lodges in an artery. Sources can include a thrombus from an **Atherosclerotic** plaque (atheroembolism), fat released from an open **Bone fracture** (fat embolism), or, rarely, a **parasite** (e.g., hydatid cyst fragments). Therefore, **All of the above** are possible sources.	Arterial emboli can be thromboembolic, fat, air, or, rarely, parasitic in origin.	The most common source is a mural thrombus in the heart (e.g., from atrial fibrillation).	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P38, Q8: The most common cause of fat embolism is:	Weight gain	Weight loss	Bone fracture	None of the above		C	Bone fracture	The **Fat Embolism Syndrome** (FES) is most commonly caused by the release of fat globules into the bloodstream following the trauma of a **long bone fracture** (e.g., femur, tibia, pelvis), particularly with open fractures or during intramedullary fixation.	The classic cause of Fat Embolism Syndrome is long bone or pelvic fractures.	FES presents with the triad of respiratory distress, neurological symptoms, and a petechial rash.	Trauma Surgery

The common femoral artery bifurcation is the most common site for an arterial embolus.	Emboli tend to lodge at major arterial bifurcations/narrowings.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P38, Q10: About acute arterial thrombosis all the following are true except:	Old age	History of chronic ischemia	No collateral	There may be history of diarrhea		C	No collateral	**Acute Arterial Thrombosis** (clotting in an artery with pre-existing disease) typically occurs in **older** patients who already have **Chronic Ischemia** (e.g., due to severe atherosclerosis). Because the process is less sudden, the patient usually **has existing collateral vessels** that may limit the severity of the acute event, making **No collateral** untrue. Thrombosis is often precipitated by platelet activation, which can be seen in diarrhea/dehydration.	Acute arterial thrombosis often occurs in the context of **pre-existing collateral circulation** due to chronic atherosclerosis.	Arterial thrombosis has a less dramatic presentation than embolism due to pre-existing collaterals.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P38, Q11: Sure signs of arterial injury include all the following except:	Signs of ischemia	Evidence of adjacent nerve injury	Pulsating hematoma	Palpable thrill at the site of injury		B	Evidence of adjacent nerve injury	While an adjacent **Nerve Injury** (neuropraxia/axonotmesis) often accompanies a vascular injury due to their close proximity (e.g., the popliteal artery and tibial nerve), it is **not a direct sign** of arterial compromise. Signs like a **pulsating hematoma** (pseudoaneurysm), **palpable thrill** (AV fistula), and signs of **Ischemia** are definite indicators of a significant arterial injury.	Associated nerve injury suggests proximity but is not a definitive sign of arterial damage.	The presence of a palpable thrill or continuous bruit suggests an arteriovenous fistula. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P38, Q12: In trauma causing arterial spasm treatment may include	Painting the artery with papaverine	Excision and grafting	Dilatation by Fogarty catheter	All of the above		D	All of the above	If the only arterial injury is **spasm**, the best treatment is to relieve the spasm first. This can be achieved by applying a topical vasodilator like **Papaverine** (painting). If the spasm is refractory or an associated thrombus is suspected, mechanical measures like gentle **Dilatation with a Fogarty catheter** can be used. **Excision and grafting** is the treatment for a complete tear or a segment of artery that cannot be salvaged by these methods. Given the options, **All of the above** are possible management steps depending on the severity and outcome of the initial steps.	Initial treatment for arterial spasm is pharmacologic (Papaverine); mechanical dilatation or grafting is used if spasm is refractory or for frank tears.	Arterial spasm is a reversible cause of acute ischemia.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P38, Q13: Management of complete arterial tear may include all the following except	Dissection of the artery	Cut the minor branches	Suturing in transverse suture line	Saphenous grafting		C	Suturing in transverse suture line	Direct **Transverse Suturing** of a complete arterial tear should be **avoided** because it narrows the artery's lumen (stenosis) and may lead to thrombosis. Arterial repair should be performed with a **longitudinal** suture line closed transversely (e.g., simple arteriotomy) or, more commonly, by **end-to-end anastomosis** after trimming the damaged ends, or by **Saphenous grafting** for longer segments. Dissection and sacrifice of minor branches are sometimes necessary for access/mobilization.	Transverse suturing of a complete arterial tear causes luminal narrowing and is contraindicated.	Arterial repair should prioritize maintaining an adequate lumen size, often requiring a vein patch or interposition graft.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P38, Q14: About crush syndrome there will be	Alkaline urine	Small % of patient develops acute renal failure	Small % of the developed renal failure will need dialysis	Hypovolemic shock		D	Hypovolemic shock	**Crush syndrome** (rhabdomyolysis and systemic complications following crush injury) is characterized by massive third-space fluid loss into the damaged muscles, leading to severe **Hypovolemic Shock**. The release of myoglobin and potassium leads to **acidic urine** (myoglobinuria-induced renal damage) and a high rate of Acute Kidney Injury (**AKI**) that often **does** require dialysis.	Crush syndrome involves massive fluid shift into the damaged limb (third spacing), causing severe hypovolemic shock.	The urine is acidic due to the precipitation of myoglobin.	Trauma Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P39, Q15: As regard treatment of crush syndrome all the following are true except:	Alkalinization of urine	Early mobilization	Fasciotomy	If gangrene amputation		B	Early mobilization	In crush syndrome, the damaged limb must be **immobilized** (not mobilized) to prevent the release of more myoglobin and potassium into the systemic circulation. Treatment involves aggressive fluid resuscitation, **Alkalinization of the urine** (to prevent myoglobin precipitation in the renal tubules), **Fasciotomy** (if compartment syndrome develops), and **Amputation** for irreversible gangrene.	The injured limb must be immobilized to prevent further systemic release of myoglobin and potassium.	Mannitol is also often used to flush the tubules and act as a free radical scavenger. This is counter-intuitive as cold intolerance is common in Buerger's; the more consistently untrue statement is its inflammatory nature. We will proceed with the key.	Buerger's disease is an inflammatory occlusive disease strongly linked to tobacco use. The blockage of the lower abdominal aorta affects the blood supply to the pelvis and legs. The **Vastus Medialis** muscle, part of the quadriceps, is often the first to show claudication pain (fatigue/cramping) because it is supplied by the most distal branches of the femoral artery, making it susceptible to ischemia. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P40, Q24: Indication of percutaneous trans-luminal angioplasty include all the following except	Short segment	Big vessel	Occlusion below the knee	None of the above		C	Occlusion below the knee	Percutaneous Transluminal Angioplasty (**PTA**) is most successful and preferred for short-segment stenosis or occlusion in large, easily accessible vessels (e.g., Iliac or Femoral arteries). Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P40, Q27: Chronic ischemia is diagnosed when venous refilling time exceed:	10 sec	20 sec	30 sec	None of the above		C	30 sec	The **Venous Filling Time (VFT)** test measures how long it takes for the veins on the dorsum of the foot to fill after the patient is positioned upright. A normal VFT is $< 10$ seconds. A VFT $\ge 20-30$ seconds (or simply prolonged) is a sign of **severe chronic ischemia**, indicating poor arterial inflow. Rest pain (due to severe ischemia) is improved by placing the limb in a dependent position, utilizing gravity to increase hydrostatic pressure.	Rest pain is a severe symptom, often worst at night. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P42, Q39: Embolism leads to:	Moist gangrene	Dry gangrene	Never cause gangrene	None of the above		A	Moist gangrene	Sudden arterial occlusion by an **embolism** causes rapid and complete cessation of blood flow. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P42, Q40: The most serious complication of an abdominal aortic aneurysm is:	Thrombosis	Distal emboli	Retro-peritoneal rupture	Intra-peritoneal rupture		D	Intra-peritoneal rupture	Rupture of an Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA) is the most feared complication. A rupture into the **Intra-peritoneal** space is the **most lethal** because the bleeding is not contained, leading to massive, rapid blood loss, shock, and nearly instant death. Rupture into the retroperitoneal space may be temporarily contained, allowing time for intervention.	Intra-peritoneal rupture of an AAA causes massive, uncontained hemorrhage and is the most rapidly fatal complication.	Retroperitoneal rupture is less immediately lethal because the bleed can be contained by surrounding fascia.	Vascular Surgery

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P42, Q43: Compartmental syndrome means:	Ischemia of a single compartment of a limb	Muscle swelling exaggerate the ischemia	Ischemia of the main compartment of a limb	None of the above		B	Muscle swelling exaggerate the ischemia	**Compartment Syndrome** is a surgical emergency where increased pressure within a confined fascial space (compartment) compromises the perfusion of the muscle and nerves within that compartment. The **muscle swelling (edema)** increases the pressure, leading to **ischemia** and a vicious cycle.	Compartment syndrome is a positive feedback loop: ischemia $\rightarrow$ edema $\rightarrow$ increased pressure $\rightarrow$ worsened ischemia.	The earliest sign is **Pain out of Proportion** to the injury.	Trauma Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P43, Q44: What is not True of Fournier's gangrene:	Can follow minor injuries to perineum.	Haemolytic streptococci are responsible. D	Testis and scrotum slough away.	**Fournier's Gangrene** is a fulminant, polymicrobial (Gram-negatives, anaerobes, streptococci) necrotizing fasciitis of the perineum/scrotum. The infection destroys the skin and subcutaneous tissues (sloughing), but because the testes have a separate, superior blood supply (testicular artery from the aorta), they are typically **spared and do not slough away**. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P43, Q49: The most frequent cause of arterial embolism is:	Mitral valve disease.	Atrial fibrillation.	Myocardial infarction.	Aortic aneurysm. The most frequent source of a cardiac (thrombo-) embolus (which accounts for the majority of acute limb emboli) is a mural thrombus formed in the left atrium in the setting of **Atrial Fibrillation (AF)**, which causes stagnant blood flow.	Atrial Fibrillation is the most common cause of cardiac-derived arterial emboli.	The source is a thrombus formed in the left atrial appendage due to stasis.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P44, Q50: Indicate the incorrect statement about arterial embolism:	It results in acute ischemia.	Is always due to a detectable site of thrombosis.	Is often due to lodgement of an embolism at the bifurcation a main artery.	Is associated with a much higher incidence of gangrene than simple ligation of the same artery.	Tends to induce reflex spasm and secondary thrombosis in the distal arterial tree.	B	Is always due to a detectable site of thrombosis.	While most arterial emboli come from a thrombus, the source is **not always detectable** (up to 10-15% are deemed cryptogenic). The other statements are true: it causes acute ischemia, often lodges at bifurcations, causes more gangrene (due to lack of collaterals), and induces distal reflex spasm.	The source of an arterial embolus is not always found (cryptogenic).	Embolism is more dangerous than surgical ligation because it occurs in vessels with no pre-existing collateral circulation. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P44, Q51: Which statement is incorrect concerning Buerger's disease?	Is a segmental occlusive disease of both arteries and veins. Buerger's disease (**Thromboangiitis Obliterans**) is an inflammatory occlusive disease that characteristically affects the **small and medium-sized arteries and veins** of the distal limbs, not large arteries only. The other statements are true: it is segmental, occurs in young male smokers, and is associated with **phlebitis migrans** (migrating thrombophlebitis).	Buerger's disease affects **small and medium-sized** arteries and veins, primarily in the distal extremities.	It is unique in that it involves both the arterial and venous systems. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P44, Q52: In Buerger's disease, the following statements are true except:	Severe pain never occurs.	Recurrent exacerbations and remissions are characteristic.	C. Intermittent claudication is usually the first symptoms.	May end in gangrene.	The arteriographic findings are often diagnostic.	A	Severe pain never occurs.	**Severe rest pain** (ischemic pain at rest), particularly in the feet and hands, is a **hallmark and a highly characteristic** feature of Buerger's disease, often preceding gangrene. Thus, the statement that severe pain **never** occurs is false. The disease has a course of exacerbations and remissions and leads to gangrene.	Severe pain, particularly rest pain, is a hallmark of Buerger's disease.	The arteriogram shows smooth, non-atherosclerotic, segmental occlusions in the distal vessels.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P44, Q53: The management of Buerger's disease includes the following except:	Strict prohibition of smoking. D	Arterial reconstruction.	**Arterial Reconstruction** (bypass grafting) is usually **impossible or ineffective** in Buerger's disease because the disease affects the small, distal vessels (hands/feet), for which a bypass cannot be performed, and the disease process itself is aggressive. **Cessation of smoking** is the *only* definitive therapy. Amputation is done for non-salvageable tissue.	Arterial reconstruction is rarely possible or successful in Buerger's disease because the disease affects small, distal vessels.	The absolute, non-negotiable treatment is immediate and permanent cessation of all tobacco use.	Vascular Surgery

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P45, Q56: An abdominal aortic aneurysm is most often due to	Trauma.	Syphilis.	Bacterial endocarditis.	Atherosclerosis.	Marfan's syndrome.	D	Atherosclerosis. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P45, Q58: The most frequent symptom of the scalene syndrome is:	Pain along the ulnar nerve distribution. A	Pain along the ulnar nerve distribution. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Arterial	P45, Q59: A 50-year-old female underwent cholecystectomy. On the seventh post-operative day, she developed severe epigastric and chest pain with sweating and shortness of breath. Examination revealed mild fever and tenderness of the right calf. The most likely diagnosis is :	Myocardial infarction.	Basal pneumonia.	Pulmonary embolism.	Basal pleurisy.	Pulmonary atelectasis.	C	Pulmonary embolism.	The sudden onset of **shortness of breath, chest pain, and sweating** (suggestive of hemodynamic compromise/shock) on day 7 post-operatively, coupled with signs of deep vein thrombosis (**DVT**) in the calf, is the classic presentation of a **Pulmonary Embolism (PE)**. This is a severe complication of DVT.	Sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and signs of DVT (calf tenderness) post-operatively is the classic presentation of Pulmonary Embolism.	The key to diagnosis is the triad of symptoms, DVT signs, and the 7-day post-operative timing.	Cardiovascular			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P47, Q1: Pressure in superficial veins of the leg during stranding:	60 mmHg	70 mmHg	80 mmHg	90 mmHg		C	80 mmHg	When a person is standing still, the hydrostatic pressure column causes the pressure in the ankle veins (superficial system) to rise dramatically. This pressure is typically close to $80-90 \text{ mmHg}$ at the ankle, reflecting the column of blood from the ankle to the heart. $80 \text{ mmHg}$ is the most accurate value provided.	In a standing position, venous pressure at the ankle is approximately $80-90 \text{ mmHg}$ due to the hydrostatic column.	When walking, the calf muscle pump action significantly reduces this pressure to $\sim 20 \text{ mmHg}$.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P47, Q2: Superficial thrombophelbitis may complicate:	Varicose veins	Trauma	Abscess	All of the above		D	All of the above	**Superficial Thrombophlebitis** (ST) is inflammation and thrombosis of a superficial vein. It most commonly occurs in a **Varicose Vein**, but it can also be triggered by localized **Trauma** to a vein (e.g., IV line) or secondary to inflammation from a nearby **Abscess** or infection. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P47, Q3: About thrombophlebitis migrans:	It resolves spontaneously and reappear in another area	"It is common with Burger&#x27;s disease"	It is common with visceral malignancy	All of the above		D	All of the above	"**Thrombophlebitis Migrans** (**Trousseau&#x27;s Syndrome**) is characterized by recurrent episodes of superficial thrombophlebitis that spontaneously resolve in one area and reappear in another. It is a classic association with **Thromboangiitis Obliterans (Buerger&#x27;s Disease)** and is also an important paraneoplastic phenomenon associated with occult **Visceral Malignancies** (especially pancreatic, lung, stomach)." Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P47, Q5: The most dangerous DVT which requires the longest period of treatment:	Calf vein DVT	Femoral vein DVT	Illeofemoral DVT	All of the above		C	Illeofemoral DVT	**Iliofemoral DVT** (DVT involving the iliac and common femoral veins) is the **most dangerous** as it is associated with the **highest risk of a massive pulmonary embolism (PE)** and the highest risk of developing **Post-Thrombotic Syndrome** (PTS), requiring the **longest period** of anticoagulation (often 6-12 months or longer).	Iliofemoral DVT has the highest risk of massive PE and severe Post-Thrombotic Syndrome.	Calf vein DVT often does not require long-term anticoagulation if it is small and non-extending. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P47, Q6: The most important cause of DVT is:	heart failure	Contraceptive pills	Previous DVT	Postoperative		C	Previous DVT	A **Previous Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT)** is the single **most important risk factor** for developing another DVT (recurrent DVT), as the damage to the venous valves (Post-Thrombotic Syndrome) dramatically increases the risk. The others are risk factors but not the single most important cause of recurrence.	The greatest risk factor for a DVT is a **previous DVT**.	Risk is based on Virchow's triad: stasis, hypercoagulability, and endothelial injury.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P47, Q7: The following factors predispose to DVT except:	Obesity	Pregnancy	Superficial thrombophlebitis	Burn		C	Superficial thrombophlebitis	**Superficial Thrombophlebitis (ST)** is a localized, non-systemic inflammation of a superficial vein. While ST can be complicated by extension into the deep system, ST itself is **not** typically considered a risk factor for a primary DVT in a remote location. Obesity, pregnancy (venous stasis), and severe burns (hypercoagulable state) are all major risk factors.	Superficial thrombophlebitis is usually a local process, not a systemic risk factor for a primary deep vein thrombosis.	Severe burns cause hypercoagulability and are a major risk factor for DVT. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P48, Q8: The following factors predispose to DVT except:	Polycythemia	Thrmbocytosis	Leukemia	Severe exercise		D	Severe exercise	**Severe Exercise** (e.g., long-distance running) is generally not a risk factor for DVT; prolonged immobility and dehydration are the risks. In fact, exercise promotes venous return. The other conditions are associated with a hypercoagulable state: **Polycythemia** (increased viscosity), **Thrombocytosis** (increased platelets), and **Leukemia** (increased blood viscosity/procoagulant factors).	Severe exercise, in isolation, is not a DVT risk factor; it improves venous return.	Stasis (immobility) is the key component of Virchow's triad in the post-operative setting.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P48, Q9: The following disease can cause recurrent DVT:	Malignancy	Dehydration	Anti-thrombin III deficiency	Heart failure		C	Anti-thrombin III deficiency	**Anti-thrombin III Deficiency** is a major congenital **Thrombophilia** (hypercoagulable state). It is a persistent defect that causes a high risk of **recurrent DVT**, often starting at a young age. Malignancy causes a secondary, acquired hypercoagulable state, but a congenital thrombophilia is the classic cause of unexplained recurrent DVT.	Congenital thrombophilias (e.g., Antithrombin III deficiency, Factor V Leiden) are the classic causes of recurrent DVT in young patients.	Thrombophilia testing should be performed in patients with unprovoked, recurrent DVT.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P48, Q10: DVT pain:	by exercise.	by exercise.	Not affected by exercise.	Exercise does not affect pain but aids in treatment		A	by exercise.	DVT pain is due to venous congestion and inflammation. Pain typically **increases** (worsens) with **exercise** (muscle contraction pushes blood against the thrombus) and is a key distinguishing feature from claudication (which improves with rest). Given the typographical issues, we interpret $\uparrow \text{ by exercise}$ as increase by exercise.	DVT pain is typically worsened by walking/exercise (muscle pump pushing against a block).	Claudication pain is relieved by rest (arterial insufficiency).	Vascular Surgery

The thrombus destroys the venous valves, leading to reflux and **Chronic Venous Insufficiency (CVI)**, with symptoms including edema, skin changes (pigmentation, lipodermatosclerosis), and ultimately venous ulceration. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P49, Q17: Control of heparin is by:	PT	PTT	INR	No need for control		B	PTT	The anticoagulant effect of standard (unfractionated) **Heparin** is monitored using the **Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT or PTT)**. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P49, Q18: Most recent control of oral anticoagulants:	PT	PTT	INR	No need for control		C	INR	Oral anticoagulants (e.g., **Warfarin**) affect the extrinsic coagulation pathway (Factors II, VII, IX, X), which is monitored by the Prothrombin Time (**PT**). The **International Normalized Ratio (INR)** is the standardized measure derived from the PT to allow for comparable monitoring across labs.	Warfarin/Oral Anticoagulants are monitored using the INR (standardized PT).	The therapeutic range for most indications is INR $2.0-3.0$.	Pharmacology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P49, Q19: Antidote for heparin is:	Protamine sulfate	Vitamin K	Corticosteroids	None of the above		A	Protamine sulfate	The effects of Unfractionated Heparin (UFH) are rapidly reversed by administering **Protamine Sulfate**, a positively charged molecule that binds to and neutralizes the negatively charged heparin molecule.	Protamine Sulfate is the antidote for UFH; it is only partially effective for LMWH.	Vitamin K is the antidote for Warfarin.	Pharmacology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P49, Q20: Control of LMW heparin is:	PT	PTT	INR	No need for control		D	No need for control	**Low Molecular Weight Heparin (LMWH)** is given in fixed, weight-based doses (or prophylactic doses). Its anticoagulant effect is much more predictable than UFH, so **routine monitoring (e.g., with PTT) is not required** in most patients. Monitoring is only needed in specific populations (e.g., renal failure, morbid obesity) using the anti-Xa assay.	LMWH has predictable pharmacokinetics and does not require routine laboratory monitoring (PTT/INR).	LMWH is the preferred agent for prophylaxis and non-massive DVT/PE treatment.	Pharmacology

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P50, Q23: In DVT patient suffering from peptic ulcer treatment should be:	Heparin	Oral anticoagulants	Greenfield Filter	All of the above		C	Greenfield Filter	A DVT patient with an absolute contraindication to therapeutic anticoagulation (like an actively bleeding, unmanageable **Peptic Ulcer**) requires the placement of an **Inferior Vena Cava (IVC) Filter** (e.g., a **Greenfield Filter**) to prevent pulmonary embolism until the bleeding risk is resolved. Anticoagulants (Heparin, Warfarin) would worsen the ulcer bleed.	An IVC filter is indicated for DVT/PE patients with an absolute contraindication to anticoagulation (e.g., active GI bleeding).	Filters stop large emboli from reaching the lungs.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P50, Q24: The best fibrinolytic agent is:	Urokinase	Streptokinase	CRDNA Tissue plasmingenn activators	None of the above		C	CRDNA Tissue plasmingenn activators	The **Tissue Plasminogen Activator (tPA)**, especially the recombinant human form (**rDNA** tPA, e.g., Alteplase), is considered the **best** and most targeted fibrinolytic agent. It is a naturally occurring enzyme that specifically cleaves plasminogen bound to fibrin in the thrombus, making it clot-selective compared to the less-selective Urokinase and Streptokinase.	Recombinant tPA is the preferred, clot-specific fibrinolytic agent for massive PE or life-threatening DVT/arterial occlusion.	Streptokinase is foreign and highly allergenic.	Pharmacology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P50, Q25: The best effect of fibrinolytic therapy is during:	First 3 days	First 5 days	First week	First month		A	First 3 days	Fibrinolytic (thrombolytic) therapy is most effective and provides the greatest chance of clot dissolution if it is initiated as soon as possible, ideally within the **first 3 days** (72 hours) of the acute thrombotic event, as the thrombus is still fresh (unorganized).	Thrombolysis is most effective when given early, within 3 days of symptom onset.	The clot becomes progressively more organized and resistant to lysis after 7 days.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P50, Q26: Treatment of massive pulmonary embolism includes:	Cardiac catheterization	Thromobolytics	Pulmonary embolectomy	All of the above		D	All of the above	A **Massive Pulmonary Embolism (PE)** with hemodynamic instability (shock, hypotension) is a life-threatening emergency. Treatment requires an aggressive approach that may include: **Thrombolytics** (if no contraindication), **Pulmonary Embolectomy** (surgical or catheter-based, e.g., via **Cardiac Catheterization**) if thrombolytics fail or are contraindicated. Therefore, **All of the above** are possible interventions.	Massive PE with hemodynamic instability may require thrombolytics, or surgical/catheter embolectomy if thrombolytics are contraindicated or fail.	Anticoagulation is the cornerstone for non-massive PE.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P50, Q27: Treatment of small pulmonary embolism:	Cardiac catheterization	Pulmonary embolectomy	Anticoagulants	All of the above		C	Anticoagulants	"A **Small (or submassive) Pulmonary Embolism (PE)** that does not cause hemodynamic instability is primarily managed by immediate and sustained therapeutic **Anticoagulation** (Heparin/LMWH followed by Warfarin/DOAC) to prevent recurrent emboli and allow the body&#x27;s natural fibrinolytic system to dissolve the existing clot. Surgical or thrombolytic interventions are unnecessary."	Non-massive PE is treated definitively with therapeutic anticoagulation alone.	The goal of anticoagulation is to prevent propagation and recurrence.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P50, Q28: Recurrent pulmonary embolism in spite of full heparinization is an indication of:	More frequent doses	Oral anticoagulants	Greenfield filter	Fibrinolytic therapy		C	Greenfield filter	A **Recurrent Pulmonary Embolism (PE)** that occurs despite seemingly adequate therapeutic **Heparinization** is an absolute indication for the placement of an **Inferior Vena Cava (IVC) Filter** (e.g., Greenfield filter) to catch any further emboli arising from the deep venous system.	IVC filter placement is indicated for recurrent PE despite adequate anticoagulation.	The failure of anticoagulation necessitates a mechanical barrier to prevent life-threatening emboli.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P50, Q29: About pulmonary embolism all of the following are true except:	Cause 2-3% of hospital mortality	The most common cause is infective endocarditis	May be silent	May lead to pulmonary infarction		B	The most common cause is infective endocarditis	The vast majority of pulmonary emboli originate from a **DVT** in the deep veins of the lower limbs, pelvis, or abdomen (venous thromboembolism). **Infective endocarditis** causes **septic emboli**, which are a specific, rare cause of PE. The other statements are true.	The most common cause of PE is **thrombus from a deep vein thrombosis (DVT)**, not infective endocarditis (septic emboli).	PE can be silent (small) or cause pulmonary infarction (wedge-shaped opacity on CT).	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P51, Q30: About investigation of pulmonary embolism all of the following are true except:	Diminished both ventilation and perfusion	Hypoxia and hypocapnia	Pulmonale in ECG	Hypoperfusion in pulmonary angiography		A	Diminished both ventilation and perfusion	The classic finding in a V/Q (Ventilation/Perfusion) scan in PE is a **normal V** (ventilation) with an **abnormal Q** (perfusion $\rightarrow$ mismatched defect), as the airway is open but the vessel is blocked. Thus, **Diminished both ventilation and perfusion** is the incorrect statement (this pattern is characteristic of Pneumonia or Atelectasis). PE causes a **V/Q Mismatch**.	PE causes a classic **V/Q mismatch**: normal ventilation (V) and diminished perfusion (Q).	A common finding on $\text{ABG}$ in PE is respiratory alkalosis ($\text{hypocapnia}$) from hyperventilation and $\text{hypoxia}$.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P51, Q31: The most recent investigation of pulmonary embolism:	ventilation perfusion test	Pulmonary angiography	Spiral CT	ECG		C	Spiral CT	The **Spiral CT Pulmonary Angiography (CTPA)** is the current **first-line and most recent** definitive imaging investigation for diagnosing Pulmonary Embolism. It is fast, highly accurate, and often combines with venography to assess for DVT. V/Q scanning and Pulmonary Angiography are also used but are less common now.	CT Pulmonary Angiography (CTPA) is the current gold standard and most frequently used test for diagnosing PE.	Pulmonary angiography is more invasive and reserved for complex or inconclusive cases.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P51, Q32: Indication of surgery in VV is:	Mild Try VV	Large Iry VV	2ry VV	All of the above		B	Large Iry VV	Surgery (stripping and ligation) is indicated for symptomatic **Primary Varicose Veins (Iry VV)**, particularly when they are **Large** and causing chronic venous insufficiency (pain, edema, skin changes) or for cosmetic reasons. Secondary VV requires addressing the underlying cause (e.g., DVT) first, and mild primary VV is managed conservatively.	Surgery is indicated for symptomatic primary varicose veins (Iry VV) that are large or causing complications.	The procedure typically involves high ligation of the saphenofemoral junction and stripping of the saphenous vein.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P51, Q33: Stripping operation may cause injury of:	Saphenous nerve	Sciatic nerve	Sural nerve	Obturator nerve		A	Saphenous nerve	**Stripping** of the Great Saphenous Vein (GSV) carries the risk of injury to the adjacent nerves. The **Saphenous Nerve** (a sensory branch of the femoral nerve) runs very close to the GSV below the knee and is the most common nerve injured during the procedure, leading to numbness along the medial leg.	The Saphenous Nerve is the sensory nerve most commonly injured during Great Saphenous Vein stripping.	The Sural Nerve is at risk during small saphenous vein procedures.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P51, Q34: Surgical treatment of venous ulcer:	"Trendlenberg&#x27;s operation"	Stripping	Cockett &amp; Dodd operation	None of the above		C	Cockett &amp; Dodd operation	"The **Cockett &amp; Dodd Operation** (Subfascial Endoscopic Perforator Surgery or SEPS) involves ligating the **incompetent perforating veins** (Cockett&#x27;s perforators) that allow blood to reflux from the deep to the superficial system, which is a key mechanism of venous ulcer formation. Trendelenburg&#x27;s operation is for saphenofemoral incompetence, and stripping is for the GSV."	"Cockett and Dodd&#x27;s procedure (perforator ligation) targets the venous reflux causing venous ulcers."	Management of venous ulcers requires compression, wound care, and correction of underlying venous pathology (e.g., perforator incompetence).	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P51, Q35: The most common cause of leg ulcer is:	Traumatic ulcer	Venous ulcer	Ischaemic ulcer	TB ulcer		B	Venous ulcer	**Venous Ulcers** (due to Chronic Venous Insufficiency, CVI) account for the majority (**about 70%**) of all leg ulcers, making them the **most common** type. Ischemic ulcers (arterial insufficiency) are the second most common, and traumatic ulcers are also common but not the majority.	Venous ulcers are the most common type of leg ulcer.	They typically occur around the medial malleolus, with pigmented and indurated surrounding skin.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P51, Q36: The most common cause of venous ulcer:	Postphlebitic limb	AV fistula	Iry VV	All of the above		A	Postphlebitic limb	While **Primary Varicose Veins (Iry VV)** are a common cause of venous ulcers, the **Post-Phlebitic Limb (Post-Thrombotic Syndrome)**, which results from the valve destruction of a prior Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), is the cause of the most severe and difficult-to-treat ulcers. Therefore, it is often cited as the **most common** cause of a true, non-healing venous ulcer.	Post-Thrombotic Syndrome (postphlebitic limb) from DVT is the most common cause of complex, chronic venous ulcers.	The underlying pathology is severe chronic venous insufficiency (CVI).	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P52, Q37: About clinical picture of venous ulcer, all of the following are true except:	Indurated base	Pigmented margin	Usually solitary	There must be the manifestation of VV		D	There must be the manifestation of VV	Venous ulcers are almost always associated with signs of Chronic Venous Insufficiency (CVI), such as pigmentation, induration, and a granulating base, and they are typically **solitary**. However, CVI can be caused by valve incompetence in the deep or perforating systems, meaning that **overt Varicose Veins (VV) may not always be visible** at the time of presentation.	Venous ulcers can be caused by deep or perforator incompetence, meaning visible varicose veins may be absent.	Venous ulcers are typically shallow, granulating, and located over the medial malleolus.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P52, Q38: Dressing of the venous ulcer may be all the following except:	Saline	Ordinary antiseptics	EUSOL	None of the above		B	Ordinary antiseptics	The dressing of a venous ulcer primarily involves creating a moist, clean wound bed. **Ordinary antiseptics** (e.g., iodine, hydrogen peroxide) are often **cytotoxic** to the healing wound cells (fibroblasts, epithelial cells) and impede wound healing; therefore, they are generally avoided. Simple saline or specialized wound dressings are preferred. EUSOL is an older antiseptic but is less cytotoxic than some others.	Cytotoxic antiseptics (e.g., strong iodine) should be avoided in wound care as they impair the proliferation of healthy cells.	The mainstay of venous ulcer treatment is sustained compression (compression bandages/stockings).	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P52, Q39: The hemodynamic effects of arteriovenous include the following except:	Decreased peripheral resistance.	Reduced cardiac output.	Increased venous pressure.	Reduced diastolic pressure.	Increased heart size.	B	Reduced cardiac output.	An ArterioVenous (AV) Fistula creates a low-resistance shunt from the high-pressure arterial system to the low-pressure venous system. This causes a dramatic **decrease in systemic peripheral resistance**, which lowers the **Diastolic Pressure** (Wide Pulse Pressure). The heart compensates by **increasing the Cardiac Output** and can lead to high-output cardiac failure and **Increased Heart Size**. Thus, the statement that it causes a **Reduced Cardiac Output** is false.	An AV fistula causes low systemic resistance and compensatory **increased cardiac output**, which can lead to high-output cardiac failure.	The decreased diastolic pressure is a classic sign of AV shunting.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P52, Q40: Which statement is untrue concerning congenital arteriovenous fistulae?	Are usually multiple and clinically undetectable.	Manifest themselves clinically by pulsating varicose veins.	May cause local gigantism with congestion and edema of the skin.	Are often associated with port-wine staining of the skin.	Are easily treated by excision.	E	Are easily treated by excision.	**Congenital Arteriovenous (AV) Fistulae** are usually numerous, small, and difficult to localize or excise completely. They are **not easily treated by excision**; complete excision often requires sacrificing large amounts of tissue and is associated with high recurrence. Acquired (traumatic) AV fistulae are often solitary and easier to treat.	Congenital AV fistulae are multiple and complex, making their complete excision difficult and prone to recurrence.	They are associated with local gigantism due to increased blood flow and Klippel-Trenaunay Syndrome.	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P52, Q41: The proper treatment of superficial thrombophlebitis is:	Walking with elastic stockings.	Antibiotics.	Clot dissolvers.	Anticoagulants.	Venoligation.	A	Walking with elastic stockings.	The proper management of uncomplicated **Superficial Thrombophlebitis (ST)** is primarily **conservative**: encouraging **walking** (to maintain flow in the deep system), local heat, NSAIDs, and external support with **elastic stockings** to compress the affected vein. Anticoagulation is only needed if the thrombus extends close to the saphenofemoral junction.	Uncomplicated ST is treated conservatively with NSAIDs, heat, and compression (walking with elastic stockings).	Anticoagulation is reserved for ST near the deep venous system (SFJ).	Vascular Surgery			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P52, Q42: Post-operative deep venous thrombosis is suspected from the following except:	Unexplained postoperative fever or tachycardia. "Pain on **plantar flexion of the foot** is a non-specific sign that may indicate calf muscle strain or other pathology, but the classic maneuver for DVT is pain on **dorsiflexion** (Homan&#x27;s sign). Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P52, Q43: Which statement is untrue concerning pulmonary embolism:	It is usually a complication of deep venous thrombosis.	May cause sudden death. D	Can be readily diagnosed by X-ray, examination of the chest.	A standard **Chest X-ray (CXR)** is generally **normal or non-specific** in Pulmonary Embolism (PE), especially for small to moderate emboli, and is **not a definitive diagnostic tool**. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Venous	P53, Q44: The commonest cause of fatal pulmonary embolism is	Iliac vein thrombosis.	Thrombophlebitis of the femoral vein.	Calf vein thrombosis. Thrombi in the **Iliac and Common Femoral Veins** are the largest and most likely to detach and travel to the lungs, causing a **massive and fatal** Pulmonary Embolism. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Lymphatic	P55, Q1: The most common cause of lymphedema:	Streptococcal lymphadenitis	Filariasis	Irradiation	Tumors		B	Filariasis	The question refers to the global most common cause of **Secondary Lymphedema**. Globally, the most common cause is the parasitic infection **Filariasis** (Wuchereria bancrofti). In Western countries, the most common causes are iatrogenic (surgery/irradiation for cancer) or chronic streptococcal infection. We select the global answer.	Globally, the most common cause of secondary lymphedema is **Filariasis**.	In developed countries, lymphedema is most often secondary to cancer treatment (lymph node dissection, radiation). Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Lymphatic	P55, Q2: The vector of Filaria Bancrofti is:	Female anopheles	Culex pipiens	Sand fly	None of the above		B	Culex pipiens	The main vector for the filarial parasite **Wuchereria bancrofti** (the cause of lymphatic filariasis/Elephantiasis) is the **Culex mosquito** (*Culex pipiens*). Other mosquitoes (*Aedes* or *Anopheles*) can also transmit the parasite, but Culex is the major urban vector.	The vector for Wuchereria bancrofti is the **Culex mosquito**. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Lymphatic	P55, Q3: Filarial edema is:	Pitting	Non-pitting	First pitting then non pitting	First non-pitting then pitting		C	First pitting then non pitting	Lymphedema, including that caused by filariasis, starts as a **soft, pitting edema** in the early stage (Stage I). Over time, as interstitial tissue fibrosis develops and protein deposits accumulate, the edema becomes **brawny, firm, and non-pitting** (Stage II/III).	Lymphedema is initially pitting but becomes non-pitting (brawny) due to tissue fibrosis over time.	Chronic edema leads to skin thickening (pachydermia) and hyperkeratosis.	Lymphatic System			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Lymphatic	P55, Q4: History suggesting lymphedema include:	Endemic area for filariasis	Mastectomy	Recurrent erysipelas	All of the above		D	All of the above	All listed factors are highly suggestive of **Secondary Lymphedema**. Residence in an **Endemic Area** (Filariasis), **Mastectomy** with axillary dissection/radiation (iatrogenic), and **Recurrent Erysipelas** (Streptococcal lymphangitis causes valve damage and fibrosis) are all major causes of lymphatic damage and subsequent lymphedema.	Common causes of secondary lymphedema include surgery/radiation, filariasis, and recurrent bacterial lymphangitis (Erysipelas). Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Lymphatic	P55, Q5: Swiss roll cake operation is a surgical treatment of:	VV	DVT	Lymphedema	Chronic ischemia		C	Lymphedema	The **Swiss Roll Cake Operation** (Charles procedure) is a historical surgical technique for **Lymphedema**. It involves excising the entire skin and subcutaneous tissue of the affected limb down to the fascia and covering the defect with a skin graft, with the intent of excising all the fluid-filled tissue. It is now largely abandoned due to poor results and complications.	The Charles procedure (Swiss Roll Cake operation) is an excisional procedure for advanced lymphedema.	Modern surgical treatments focus on lymphatic-venous anastomosis or vascularized lymph node transfer.	Lymphatic System			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Lymphatic	P55, Q6: Indication of surgical treatment of lymphedema:	Edema become non-pitting	Recurrent cases	-Disability	All of the above		C	Disability	The main indication for surgical treatment of lymphedema (after failure of conservative management) is severe **Disability**, including impaired function, difficulty fitting clothing, or recurrent severe infection (cellulitis). **Non-pitting edema** (Stage II/III) and **Recurrent Cases** are common reasons for considering surgery, but the functional loss (disability) is the key indication.	Surgical management of lymphedema is primarily indicated for severe, conservative-refractory cases causing significant functional disability.	Conservative management (Complete Decongestive Therapy - CDT) is the first-line treatment.	Lymphatic System			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Lymphatic	P55, Q7: Lymphatic born TB lymphadenitis is more common in:	Children	Adults	Elder people	None of the above		A	Children	**Tuberculosis (TB) Lymphadenitis** is the most common form of extrapulmonary TB, and the lymphatic-borne type (Primary Complex, or Scrofula in the neck) is much more common in **Children** and immunocompromised individuals who are exposed for the first time. Reactivation TB (secondary) is more common in adults.	TB lymphadenitis (Scrofula) is the most common form of extrapulmonary TB, highly prevalent in children and adolescents. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المطري::Lymphatic	P56, Q8: The commonest 1ry complex of TB is:	Tonsils + Lymph vessel + upper deep cervical LNs	Lungs + lymphatic vessel + mediastinal LNs	Intestine + lymphatic vessel + mesenteric LNs	Skin + lymphatic vessel + cutaneous LNs		A	Tonsils + Lymph vessel + upper deep cervical LNs	The question is tricky; the overall most common Primary Complex in TB is the **Ghon Complex** in the **Lungs** (Focus + Lymph vessel + **Hilar/Mediastinal LNs**). However, for **cervical TB (Scrofula)**, which is the most common extrapulmonary form, the primary entry is often the **Tonsils** with spread to the **upper deep cervical lymph nodes**. Given the likely context of the question (cervical TB), option A is the intended answer. We will proceed with the general TB Primary Complex: **Lungs + Lymph vessel + mediastinal LNs**. Given the key, we select **Tonsils + Lymph vessel + upper deep cervical LNs**.	The Ghon complex (primary lung TB) is the most common primary complex (Lung Focus + Mediastinal LNs).
