C	Unilateral red scaly nipple	"Paget&#x27;s disease of the nipple presents as a chronic, eczematous, scaly, or ulcerated lesion of the nipple-areola complex, almost always unilateral."	Associated with underlying DCIS or invasive carcinoma in &gt;95% of cases.	Step 1: Paget cells are large intraepithelial adenocarcinoma cells with clear halos (PAS positive).	Breast &gt; Pathology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Breast part 2	P2, Q47. Lobular carcinoma of breast All true except:	Is more common than ductal carcinoma.	In situ state is considered risk factor for malignancy.	With in situ carcinoma mammography of both breasts is mandatory.	Usually bilateral and multicentric.		A	Is more common than ductal carcinoma.	Invasive Ductal Carcinoma is the most common type (~80%). Invasive Lobular Carcinoma accounts for only 10-15%. Thus, statement A is false.	"Lobular carcinoma often lacks E-cadherin, leading to a &#x27;single file&#x27; limitation of cells."	Step 1: E-cadherin loss is the hallmark molecular feature of Lobular Carcinoma (LCIS and Invasive).	Breast &gt; Pathology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Breast part 2	P2, Q48. As regard incidence of cancer breast all correct except:	Most common female malignancy.	Rare in nulliparous females.	Very rare below 20 years.	Males are rare to be affected but with grave prognosis.		B	Rare in nulliparous females.	Nulliparity is a *risk factor* for breast cancer (increased exposure to uninterrupted estrogen cycles). Therefore, it is more common, not rare, in nulliparous females compared to multiparous.	Breastfeeding and early full-term pregnancy are protective.	Step 1: Risk factors = Estrogen exposure (Early menarche, Late menopause, Nulliparity, Obesity).	Breast &gt; Epidemiology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Breast part 2	P3, Q49. Massive swellings of the breast include all the following except:	phylloides tumor	Atrophic scirrhous carcinoma.	Diffuse hypertrophy.	Giant fibroadenoma.		B	Atrophic scirrhous carcinoma.	"Scirrhous carcinoma is characterized by a desmoplastic stromal reaction that causes fibrosis, contraction, and nipple retraction, resulting in a shrinking or hard, fixed mass rather than a &#x27;massive swelling&#x27;."	Phyllodes tumors can grow rapidly to a massive size.	Step 2 CK: A rapidly growing large breast mass is likely a Phyllodes tumor or Giant Fibroadenoma.	Breast &gt; Pathology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Breast part 2	P3, Q50. Tamoxifen use in breast cancer causes all, Except:	Decreases recurrence in affected breast.	Decreases incidence in contralateral breast.	Increased incidence of endometrial cancer.	Increased incidence of myocardial infarction.		A	Decreases recurrence in affected breast.	The question likely asks for the *therapeutic benefit* as the 'exception' to the list of potential adverse risks, OR implies which is 'false' among side effects (though A is true). Given the key is A, it highlights the primary indication (reducing recurrence) amidst side effects/risks. Note: Option D (Increased MI) is medically false (Tamoxifen improves lipid profiles), but A is the key provided.	Tamoxifen is a SERM: Antagonist in breast, Agonist in uterus/bone.	Step 1: Tamoxifen increases risk of Endometrial Cancer and Thromboembolism (DVT/PE).	Breast &gt; Pharmacology			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Breast part 2	P3, Q51. In curable breast cancer in premenopausal females may be treated by the following except:	Local mastectomy	Radiotherapy	Estrogen administration	Chemotherapy	Tamoxifen	C	Estrogen administration	Administering estrogen is contraindicated in breast cancer treatment (as most are estrogen-driven). Treatments include surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, and anti-estrogens (Tamoxifen).	Ovarian suppression (GnRH agonists) is also used in premenopausal women.	Step 2 CK: Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT) is contraindicated in women with a history of breast cancer.	Breast &gt; Management			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Breast part 2	P4, Q52. The most useful investigation for breast with prosthesis is:	Mammography.	US.	FNAB.	MRI.	Hormonal receptors.	D	MRI. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Breast part 2	P4, Q53. Triple assessment includes the following except:	Clinical evaluation.	Imaging.	Laboratory investigations.	Cytology.		C	Laboratory investigations.	Triple assessment consists of: 1. Clinical Exam, 2. Imaging (Mammography/US), and 3. Pathology (Biopsy/Cytology). Routine lab work is not a component of the diagnostic triad for a breast lump.	Diagnostic accuracy of triple assessment is &gt;99%.	Step 2 CK: If any component of the triple test is discordant (e.g., suspicious mass but benign biopsy), excisional biopsy is required.	Breast &gt; Diagnosis			
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Breast part 2	P4, Q54. Etiology of breast abscess includes:	Mostly in lactating women.	Due to Staph from baby mouth.	Bad hygiene.	All of the above. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Breast part 2	P4, Q55. Signs of pus formation are the following except:	Hectic fever.	Edema of overlying skin.	No response to medical treatment for 48 hours. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Breast part 2	P5, Q56. Best treatment of chronic breast abscess is:	Prolonged antibiotic courses.	Repeated aspiration.	Incision ond drainage. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Breast part 2	P11, Q80. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Head & neck part 1	P8, Q31. What percentage of submandibular tumors is malignant?	20 per cent	30 per cent	40 per cent	50 per cent	60 per cent.	D	50 per cent. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Head & neck part 1	P12, Q48. A patient is diagnosed to have malignant metastasis in right upper deep cervical lymph nodes. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Head & neck part 2	P1, Q56. The anterior 2/3 of the tongue receives common sensation through .nerve:	Glossopharyngeal.	Chorda tympani of facial.	Lingual.	Hypoglossal.		C	Lingual.	General somatic sensation (pain, touch, temperature) for the anterior 2/3 of the tongue is carried by the Lingual nerve (V3). Taste is carried by the Chorda Tympani (CN VII).	Posterior 1/3 sensation and taste are both CN IX.	Step 1: Hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) provides motor innervation to all tongue muscles except Palatoglossus (CN X).				> Anatomy and Head Neck

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Head & neck part 2	P2, Q58. Salivary gland stones are commonest in...........glands:	Submandibular.	Parotid.	Sublingual.	Minor.		A	Submandibular. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Head & neck part 2	P2, Q59. In bidigital examination, they are better felt from inside.	Pain may refer to tongue or teeth.		A	Neck US may be a useful investigation.	The question asks for the 'incorrect' statement, and the provided key is A. However, medically, Ultrasound *is* a useful non-invasive investigation. The likely intended logic is that 90% are radiopaque (B) and palpable (C), so plain X-ray or exam is standard, potentially implying US is less primary in older teaching. Or A implies it's *not* useful? We follow the key A as the exception.	Standard of care: Non-contrast CT is the gold standard today. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Head & neck part 2	P2, Q60. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Hernia	P1, Q1. Which of the following is not a predisposing factor for a hernia?	Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease	Obesity	Urinary stones	Pregnancy	Peritoneal dialysis.	C	Urinary stones	Conditions that increase intra-abdominal pressure predispose to hernias. While straining from bladder outlet obstruction (e.g., BPH) is a factor, urinary stones themselves (unless causing significant obstruction/straining) are less directly a global risk factor compared to COPD (cough), Obesity, Pregnancy, and Peritoneal Dialysis (constant distension). Note: The source key identifies C as the answer.	Risk factors for hernia: Chronic cough (COPD), Straining (Constipation, Prostatism), Obesity, Pregnancy, Ascites, Previous incision.	Step 2 CK: Indirect inguinal hernias are the most common type in both men and women.				> Hernia surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Hernia	P1, Q2. Which of the following are more common in multiparous women?	Indirect inguinal hernia	Lumbar hernia	Umbilical	Direct inguinal hernia		C	Umbilical	Umbilical and paraumbilical hernias are frequently acquired in adult women due to the stretching of the abdominal wall and linea alba during repeated pregnancies (multiparity) and obesity.	Paraumbilical hernias are much more common in women (F:M ratio ~5:1).	Step 1: Indirect inguinal hernias are congenital (patent processus vaginalis); Direct are acquired (weakness of transversalis fascia).				> Hernia surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Hernia	P1, Q3. Which of the following is not a complication of an inguinal hernia?	Irreducibility	Inflammation	Strangulation	Obstruction	Bleeding.	E	Bleeding.	Common complications of hernias include irreducibility (incarceration), obstruction, strangulation, and rarely inflammation. Spontaneous bleeding is not a typical complication of the hernia itself.	Strangulation is a surgical emergency involving compromised blood supply.	Step 2 CK: Signs of strangulation: Fever, Tachycardia, Leukocytosis, Redness over the hernia.				> Hernia surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Hernia	P1, Q4. Which of the following is not true in relation to strangulated hernias?	They present with local and then generalized abdominal pain and vomiting.	A normal hernia can strangulate at any time.	This is more common in femoral hernia.	They can be reliably excluded in irreducible hernias on clinical examination.	They require urgent surgery.	D	They can be reliably excluded in irreducible hernias on clinical examination.	It is difficult to reliably exclude strangulation in an irreducible hernia based solely on clinical examination, especially in the early stages or in obese patients. A high index of suspicion is required.	Femoral hernias have the highest rate of strangulation due to the narrow femoral ring.	Step 2 CK: Never attempt to manually reduce a strangulated hernia (risk of reduction en masse or perforation).				> Hernia surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Hernia	P2, Q5. Regarding the anatomy of the inguinal canal, which of the following are true?	In infants the internal and external rings are almost superimposed.	The inferior epigastric vessels lie posterior and lateral to the internal ring.	The inguinal canal is about 10 cm long and is directed downwards, medially and forwards.	The internal ring is a U-shaped opening in the external oblique aponeurosis 1.25 cm above the mid-inguinal point.		A	In infants the internal and external rings are almost superimposed.	In infants, the inguinal canal is very short, and the deep (internal) and superficial (external) rings are nearly superimposed, which facilitates the passage of the testis. The canal lengthens with growth.	Inferior epigastric vessels lie medial to the internal ring.	Step 1: Deep inguinal ring is a defect in the Transversalis Fascia. Superficial ring is in the External Oblique Aponeurosis.				> Abdomen Anatomy

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Hernia	P7, Q25. Hernial sac can be identified by all of the following EXCEPT:	Opaque.	Pearly white in color.	Containing the vas difference &amp; spermatic a. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Hernia	P8, Q29. Irreducible hernia can be presented by any of the following EXCEPT:	Not tense.	Not tender.	No sign of I.O.	Lost impulse on cough. Classic signs of strangulation are: Irreducibility, Tenderness, Tension, and Loss of expansile cough impulse.	Systemic signs: Tachycardia, Fever.	Step 2 CK: Urgent surgery is indicated; do not delay for imaging.				> Hernia surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Hernia	P8, Q31. The most common type of hernia liable for strangulation is:	Incisional.	Para-umbilical.	Femoral.	Direct.		C	Femoral.	Femoral hernias have the highest risk of strangulation (up to 40%) due to the rigid borders of the femoral ring (Lacunar ligament, Inguinal ligament, Pectineal ligament).	Femoral canal is narrow and unyielding.	Step 2 CK: Always refer femoral hernias for elective repair.				> Hernia surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Hernia	P8, Q32. The commonest hernia type that can be seen strangulated is:	Incisional	Inguinal.	Para-umbilical.	Femoral.		B	Inguinal.	While femoral hernias have the highest *rate* of strangulation, Inguinal hernias are so much more common overall that they account for the highest *absolute number* of strangulated hernias seen in practice.	Inguinal &gt; Femoral &gt; Umbilical in frequency.	Step 2 CK: Indirect inguinal strangulates more often than Direct.				> Epidemiology surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Hernia	P9, Q33. The pathological sequence of the strangulated hernia includes all of the following except:	Impeded venous return.	Intestinal Obstruction. Intestinal Obstruction is a clinical consequence/presentation, not a step in the tissue pathology of the strangulated segment itself (and Richter&#x27;s hernia can strangulate without IO)."	Venous flow stops first.	Step 1: Ischemia leads to perforation and peritonitis.				> GI Pathology
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Hernia	"P9, Q34. During the hernial repair, all the following are &#x27;wrong&#x27; except:"	Viable intestine is returned back to the abdomen. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Hernia	P9, Q35. The ONLY cause of last expansile impulses on cough during hernia examination is:	Irreducible hernia.	Obstructed hernia.	Strangulated hernia. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Plastic surgery	P1, Q1. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Plastic surgery	P1, Q2. About 3rd degree (full thickness) burn:	Completely painless	No graft is needed	Healing occurs from skin appendages	Usually dry, white or block		D	Usually dry, white or block	Full-thickness (3rd degree) burns destroy the entire epidermis and dermis, appearing dry, leathery, and white, brown, or black (eschar). They are painless due to nerve destruction, unlike the option A which implies the *entire* burn scenario, but D is the descriptive clinical appearance. Note: Key D is selected.	3rd Degree Burn = Insensate, dry, does not blanch.	Step 2 CK: Full thickness burns require excision and grafting; they do not heal by primary intention.				> Burns surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Plastic surgery	P1, Q3. In the rule of nines:	Front of trunk = 27%	Head and neck = 18%	Each lower limb = 18%	All of the above		C	Each lower limb = 18%	In the adult Rule of Nines, each lower extremity accounts for 18% of the total body surface area (9% anterior + 9% posterior). The front of the trunk is 18%, and the head and neck are 9%.	Rule of Nines (Adult): Head 9%, Arm 9%, Leg 18%, Chest 18%, Back 18%, Perineum 1%. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Plastic surgery	P1, Q4. Biological dressings reduce fluid loss and pain. Occlusive dressings are standard for limb burns. The exposure method is often used for the face and perineum to facilitate care and reduce infection risk from closed dressings in difficult areas.	Parkland Formula: 4 mL x kg x %TBSA (Give 1/2 in first 8 hours).	Step 1: Inhalation injury requires early intubation; look for singed nasal hairs.				> Burns surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Plastic surgery	P2, Q5. About healing intentions:	Ulcers heal by first intention	Second intention gives better cosmoses than first	First intention occurs on clean cut edges without gaps or tissue loss	All of the above		C	First intention occurs on clean cut edges without gaps or tissue loss	Primary intention healing occurs when wound edges are approximated (sutured) with minimal tissue loss, leading to rapid healing and minimal scarring. Ulcers heal by secondary intention (granulation), which leads to worse cosmesis.	Primary Intention = Sutures/Staples; Secondary Intention = Granulation; Tertiary = Delayed Primary. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Plastic surgery	P2, Q6. Corticosteroids inhibit collagen synthesis and inflammatory response, delaying healing.	Vitamin C is essential for collagen hydroxylation.	Step 2 CK: Smoking is a major risk factor for wound dehiscence and failure.				> Healing surgery Wound

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Plastic surgery	P4, Q13. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Plastic surgery	P4, Q15. Metabolic changes after burning are due to the following except :	The endocrine responses to injury. Burns destroy the skin barrier, leading to *increased* heat loss (evaporative cooling) and hypermetabolism to maintain body temperature. Reduced heat loss is incorrect.	Burn patients are hypermetabolic and require high calorie intake.	Step 2 CK: Keep burn room temperature high to prevent hypothermia.				> Burns surgery

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Plastic surgery	P5, Q17. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Plastic surgery	P6, Q24. A 6 year old boy has a 2 cm lesion on Right cheek. The lesion has been enhancing since birth. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Plastic surgery	"P7, Q26. Which of the following is true about Langer&#x27;s line of the skin?"	These are in the direction of muscle fibres. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Testis	P10, Q39. In testicular tumours, which of the following statements is false? "Wait, Seminomas *do* spread via lymphatics (True). Teratomas (NSGCTs) occur earlier (20s) than Seminomas (30s-40s) (True). Lymphatic spread to Para-aortic (True). Mass inseparable from testis = Tumor (True). There seems to be a conflict. However, looking at the key, it selects D. Perhaps it implies Seminomas spread *only* by lymphatics (which is mostly true) vs NSGCT (Hematogenous). Or maybe the error is in the age range (Seminoma peak 35-45, Teratoma 20-30). Let&#x27;s re-read D. &#x27;Seminomas usually spread via the lymphatics.&#x27; This is a TRUE statement. Let&#x27;s look at C: Teratomas (NSGCT) 3rd decade (20s), Seminomas 4th decade (30s). This is also TRUE. A is TRUE. B is TRUE. The key D might suggest a nuance or error in the source. Re-evaluating based on common trick questions: Maybe Seminoma spreads via blood late? Yes. Maybe the question implies &#x27;early&#x27; vs &#x27;late&#x27;. But if forced to pick D based on the key: The key says D. I will extract D and note the potential logic conflict, but adhere to the key. *Correction*: Actually, in some older texts, the distinction might be phrased differently. However, standard USMLE: Seminoma = Lymphatic. Chorio/Embryonal = Hematogenous. If D is false, it means Seminoma does *not* spread via lymphatics? That&#x27;s wrong. Let&#x27;s look at C again. 3rd decade = 20-29. 4th decade = 30-39. This fits. I will follow the provided key D, even if medically debatable/unclear."	Seminoma is the most common testicular tumor. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 1	P1, Q1. About Claudication which of the following statements are true?	Intermittent claudication may be present at rest. Intermittent claudication distance is usually inconsistent on a day-to-day basis for a given patient.	Intermittent claudication is thought to be due to nerve compression in the leg muscle compartments.	C	Intermittent claudication is most commonly felt in the calf.	Intermittent claudication is muscle pain brought on by exercise and relieved by rest. The calf muscles (gastrocnemius and soleus) are most commonly affected because the superficial femoral artery is the most common site of stenosis.	Claudication is reproducible at a specific walking distance (claudication distance).	Step 2 CK: Differentiate from neurogenic claudication (spinal stenosis) which is relieved by bending forward, not just stopping.				> surgery Vascular
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 1	P1, Q2. About Investigations the following statements are true except?	Doppler ultrasound works on the basis of a frequency shift when sound waves hit moving red blood cells.	"ABPI means &#x27;ankle brachial pulsatility index."	An ABPI of greater than 0.9 is probably normal.	Duplex scans are a combination of wave form analysis and B-mode ultrasound.	Carotid surgery is often performed on the basis of duplex scans without further imaging.	B	"ABPI means &#x27;ankle brachial pulsatility index."	"ABPI stands for Ankle Brachial Pressure Index (or Ankle Brachial Index), not &#x27;pulsatility index&#x27;. It is the ratio of systolic blood pressure at the ankle to that in the arm."	Normal ABPI is 0.9 to 1.3.	Step 2 CK: ABPI &lt; 0.9 indicates PAD; ABPI &gt; 1.3 indicates non-compressible calcified vessels (e.g., diabetics).				> surgery Vascular

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 1	P6, Q23. A 55-year-old woman gives a history of tiredness, aching, and a feeling of heaviness in the left lower leg for the past 3 months. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 2	P1, Q41. Evaluation of the natural history of abdominal aortic aneurysms in patients who are followed without any surgical procedure indicates that:	Approximately 20% are alive at the end of 5 years.	Seventy-five per cent of patients succumb by the end of the first year.	Aortic rupture is quite common in this group, occurring in more than half by the second year.	None of the above.		A	Approximately 20% are alive at the end of 5 years.	The natural history of untreated large abdominal aortic aneurysms is poor, with a high mortality rate primarily due to rupture. Approximately 20% 5-year survival is a cited statistic for untreated patients.	Rupture risk increases exponentially with diameter &gt;5.5 cm.	Step 2 CK: Repair is indicated for AAA &gt;5.5 cm in men, &gt;5.0 cm in women, or symptomatic.				> surgery Vascular
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 2	P1, Q42. The appropriate treatment in most situations of an aortic abdominal graft that has become infected is:	Intravenous antibiotics and observation for future complication s. D	Excision of the entire graft and insertion of ax illobifemoral grafts.	Infected aortic grafts typically require complete excision of the foreign material to eradicate infection. Revascularization is achieved via an extra-anatomic route (axillobifemoral bypass) to avoid placing a new graft in the infected bed. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 2	P1, Q43. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 2	P3, Q51. A patient presents with a gunshot wound of the mid-neck. Although drunk, he exhibits no lateralizing neurologic signs. After control of his airway is achieved, he is taken directly to the operating room for control of hemorrhage. The common carotid artery has a 2-cm. destroyed segment. A	Vein graft replacement of the common carotid artery.	In a patient with no neurologic deficit, the goal is to restore flow to prevent stroke. Ligation is reserved for patients with dense coma/no prograde flow (controversial) or technical impossibility.	Step 2 CK: Prosthetic grafts should be avoided in contaminated fields.				> surgery trauma

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 2	P4, Q55. Which of the following statements about lytic agents is/are true? Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 2	P4, Q56. Which of the following is not an indication for postoperative heparinization ?	Suspected venous thrombosis. D	Dissolution of residual thrombus after balloon thromboembolectomy.	"Heparin is an anticoagulant; it prevents *new* clot formation or propagation. It does *not* dissolve (lyse) existing thrombus. Step 2 CK: Do not anticoagulate (may destabilize more plaque). Treat supportively.				> medicine Vascular

The following is/are true:	The most likely source of an arterial embolus is from intracardiac thrombus on a previous MI	If atrial fibrillation (AF) is present, it is known that chronic AF is less likely to produce embolism than paroxysmalAF	Currently, the most common cause of AF is ischemic rather than rheumatic heart disease	Aspirin is more effective than Coumadin in AF for reducing risk of stroke and cardiovascular mortality		C	Currently, the most common cause of AF is ischemic rather than rheumatic heart disease	Ischemic heart disease and hypertension are the most common causes of Atrial Fibrillation in developed countries (replacing Rheumatic disease). AF is the leading source of emboli.	Warfarin/DOACs are superior to Aspirin for AF stroke prevention.	Step 1: Mural thrombus forms in the left atrium or ventricle.				> Cardiology medicine
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 2	P9, Q75. A 67-year-old man with acute popliteal arterial embolism has a negative cardiac echo for source of the thrombus. The following is/are true:	Most likely non-cardiac source is a thoracic aortic aneurysm	Embolism is more common from femoral than popliteal arterial aneurysms	Emboli from popliteal aneurysms are often clinically silent	Embolism is rare from subclavian artery aneurysms		C	Emboli from popliteal aneurysms are often clinically silent	Wait, Popliteal aneurysms frequently present with distal embolization (to tibial vessels) which can be silent initially (microemboli) until runoff is compromised (acute ischemia). AAA is the most common aneurysm, but Popliteal is the most common *peripheral* one and a major source of non-cardiac emboli. Note: Key says C. (A is false - AAA is more common source than thoracic. B is false - Popliteal embolizes more often).	Popliteal aneurysms are bilateral in 50% of cases.	Step 2 CK: Always check for AAA if Popliteal aneurysm is found.				> surgery Vascular
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 2	P9, Q76. Which of the following is/are appropriate candidates for exercise testing? Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 2	P10, Q79. Which of the following is/are true regarding treatment of diabetes? Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 2	P10, Q80. Pressure in superficial veins of the leg during standing:	60 mmHg	70 mmHg	80 mmHg	90 mmHg		C	80 mmHg	In the upright position, the hydrostatic pressure column from the heart to the ankle is approximately 80-100 mmHg. The venous pressure in the foot/ankle veins is therefore high (around 80-90 mmHg) when standing motionless. Key selects C (80).	Walking activates the calf muscle pump, reducing ambulatory venous pressure to &lt; 30 mmHg.	Step 1: Valves prevent retrograde flow and lower pressure during walking.				> Physiology Vascular

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::المنصورة محدث::Vascular part 2	P11, Q83. About phlegmasia cerulae dolens all the following ore true except:	There is massive iliofemoral DVT	Severe congestion and cyanosis of lower limb	Treated by anticoagulants	May lead to venous gangrene		C	Treated by anticoagulants	"Phlegmasia Cerulea Dolens (painful blue edema) is a limb-threatening emergency caused by massive DVT obstructing nearly all venous outflow. It leads to compartment syndrome and arterial compromise (venous gangrene). Simple anticoagulation is often *insufficient*; aggressive treatment like catheter-directed thrombolysis or thrombectomy is usually indicated to save the limb. Thus, &#x27;Treated by anticoagulants&#x27; (implying *only* or *sufficiently*) is the exception/false statement in the context of avoiding gangrene." Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2021	P2, Q1. Steroid is a factor that impairs wound healing; this impairment occurs due to:	Decreasing angiogenesis and macrophage migration	Decreasing platelet plug integrity	Increasing release of lysosomal enzymes	Increasing fibrinolysis	Vasodilatation	A	Decreasing angiogenesis and macrophage migration	Corticosteroids inhibit the inflammatory phase of wound healing by suppressing macrophage migration (essential for growth factor release) and inhibiting angiogenesis and collagen synthesis.	Vitamin A can partially reverse the inhibitory effects of corticosteroids on wound healing.	REMEMBER: Steroids stop the 'Construction Crew' (Macrophages and Angiogenesis) from arriving at the site.				> Healing surgery Wound
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2021	P2, Q2. Wound management one of the main topics in surgery; which of the following statement is true concerning the clinical management of an open wound?	A wet-to-dry dressing is the most optimal form of wound management	A moist occlusive dressing promotes epithelialization and reduces pain	Irrigation of the wound disrupts epithelialization therefore inhibiting the healing process	Povidine iodine is the best antiseptic solution	The protein rich plasma exudate covering the open wound facilitates healing	B	A moist occlusive dressing promotes epithelialization and reduces pain	Moist wound healing is the standard of care; it accelerates epithelialization by allowing cells to migrate easily across the wound surface and prevents tissue desiccation.	Wet-to-dry dressings are non-selective mechanical debridement tools that can damage healthy tissue and are painful; they are not 'optimal' for clean wounds.	Epithelial cells cannot migrate over dry crust (scab); they require a moist bed.				> Healing surgery Wound
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2021	P2, Q3. Shock can best be defined as:	Anemia	Hypotension	Hypoperfusion	Hypoxemia	Hypovolemia	C	Hypoperfusion	Shock is defined physiologically as inadequate tissue perfusion (hypoperfusion) resulting in an inability to meet cellular metabolic demands.	Hypotension is a late sign of shock (Class III hemorrhage), not the definition.	Lactate levels are a key marker of global tissue hypoperfusion.				> Shock surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2021	P2, Q4. Septic shock is a type of shock that commonly observed in surgical field; signs associated with early sepsis include:	Cutaneous vasodilation	Decreased cardiac output	Hypoglycemia	Increased arteriovenous oxygen difference	Respiratory acidosis	A	Cutaneous vasodilation	Early septic shock ('Warm Shock') is characterized by hyperdynamic changes: tachycardia, increased cardiac output, and cutaneous vasodilation (warm, flushed skin) due to inflammatory mediators.	Late septic shock ('Cold Shock') presents with decreased cardiac output and vasoconstriction.	Hyperglycemia (insulin resistance) is common in early sepsis, not hypoglycemia.				> Shock surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2021	P2, Q5. Severe hemorrhage is a medical catastrophe; what percentage of blood volume must be lost in healthy patients before hypotension occurs?	5-10%	10-30%	20-30%	30-40%	40-50%	D	30-40%	Systolic hypotension is a sign of Class III Hemorrhage, which corresponds to a blood volume loss of 30-40% (approx. 1500-2000 mL).	Class I (<15%) and Class II (15-30%) hemorrhage are compensated by tachycardia and vasoconstriction, maintaining systolic BP.	Class III hemorrhage is the 'tipping point' where compensation fails and hypotension appears.				> surgery trauma
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2021	P3, Q6. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2021	P6, Q24. Severe trauma	B	Neurofibroma	Neurofibroma is a benign nerve sheath tumor and is not an indication for amputation unless there is massive malignant transformation or deformity rendering the limb useless (rare).	"Indications for amputation include &#x27;Dead&#x27; (gangrene), &#x27;Deadly&#x27; (sepsis/malignancy), and &#x27;Dead useless&#x27; (severe trauma/contracture/rest pain)."	Gas gangrene (Clostridium) requires immediate debridement, often resulting in amputation to save life.				> Orthopedics surgery

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::Exam 32--39	P1, Q4. Regarding varicose veins of the lower limb; which of the following are true?	Handheld Doppler provides an accurate assessment of sapheno-popliteal competence.	Right leg varicose veins are more common than left leg varicose veins.	The prevalence of varicose veins is twice more common in women than in men	Varicose veins may cause venous claudication.	Varicose veins may be associated with the fOXC2 gene. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::Exam 32--39	P1, Q5. Regarding lymphedema; all of the following are a risk factor except:	Air travel. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::Exam 32--39	P1, Q6. A 55-year-old woman gives a history of tiredness, aching, and a feeling of heaviness in the left lower leg for the past 3 months. These symptoms are relieved by leg elevation. She is also awakened frequently by calf and foot cramping, which is relieved by leg elevation, walking, or massage. On physical examination there are superficial varicosities, non-pitting edema, and a slightly painful, 2 cm. diameter superficial ulcer 5 cm. above and behind the left medial malleolus. What is the most appropriate diagnosis?	Arterial insufficiency.	Deep venous insufficiency	Isolated symptomatic varicose veins.	Incompetent perforating veins.	Superficial lymphatic obstruction.	B	Deep venous insufficiency	The clinical picture of heavy aching legs relieved by elevation, night cramps, non-pitting edema (chronic), and a medial malleolar ulcer is classic for Chronic Venous Insufficiency (Post-thrombotic syndrome).	"Venous ulcers are typically located in the &#x27;Gaiter area&#x27; (medial supramalleolar), have irregular edges, and a granulating base."	Arterial ulcers are painful, punched-out, and located on toes/pressure points; pain is worsened by elevation.				> surgery Vascular
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::Exam 32--39	P2, Q7. Do not rub the tissue or use dry heat (fire/radiator) as this causes further damage.	Debridement is delayed until demarcation is clear (weeks to months).				> surgery trauma
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::Exam 32--39	P2, Q8. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::Exam 32--39	P2, Q10. A child 10-years old presented with manifestations of testicular torsion; regarding this condition;	CT scan should be done to confirm the diagnosis	It is most common above 25 years of age.	Inversion of the testis and a transverse lie are not a cause.	The typical symptom is a scrotal swelling	The emergency operation should be done, and fixation of testis on other side must be done.	E	The emergency operation should be done, and fixation of testis on other side must be done.	Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency; if viable, the testis is detorsed and fixed (orchiopexy), and the contralateral testis is also fixed to prevent future torsion.	Diagnosis is clinical; Doppler ultrasound is used only if diagnosis is equivocal, but should not delay surgery.	Bell-clapper deformity (high insertion of tunica vaginalis) predisposes to torsion.				> surgery Urology
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::Exam 32--39	P2, Q11. Which of the following hernias follows the path of the spermatic cord within the cremaster muscle? Direct inguinal hernias bulge through the posterior wall (transversalis fascia) separate from the cord.	Indirect hernias are lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels.				> Hernia surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::Exam 32--39	P2, Q12. Regarding epigastric hernia; which of the following statements is correct about the diagnosis and management? They typically contain preperitoneal fat (fatty hernia of linea alba) rather than bowel, and are often irreducible but rarely strangulated.	Pain is often disproportionate to size due to fat strangulation.				> Hernia surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::Exam 32--39	P3, Q13. A 40-years old male patient presented with strangulated hernia; the hernia clinically is:	Irreducible swelling.	Red &amp; hot	Tender &amp; tense. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::Exam 32--39	P3, Q14. Benign breast diseases are common conditions; all of the following concepts are true as regard, except:	Fibroadenoma is the commonest benign tumor. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::Exam 32--39	P3, Q15. Which of the following conditions have an increased risk of breast carcinoma? A	Atypical ductal or lobular hyperplasia	Atypical hyperplasia (ductal or lobular) is a proliferative lesion with atypia, increasing the risk of breast cancer by 4-5 fold.	Simple cysts and fibroadenomas (without complex features) are non-proliferative and do not significantly increase risk.	Lobular Carcinoma In Situ (LCIS) is a risk marker for bilateral breast cancer.				> Breast surgery

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::Exam 32--39	P4, Q21. Concerning inhalation injury of burn; which of the following statements are true? Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End semester 2023	P1, Q4. A 55-year-old woman gives a history a feeling of heaviness in the left lower leg for the past 3 months. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End semester 2023	P1, Q5. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End semester 2023	P1, Q6. Concerning abdominal aortic aneurysms, all the following statements are true except:	Are often associated with hypertension. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End semester 2023	P2, Q7. Patient 41 years presented by stab wound in the inguinal region, on examination there was pallor, weakness and numbness of left lower limb. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End semester 2023	P2, Q10. Precocious puberty is a character of which of the following:	Leydig cell tumor.	Lymphoma	Sertoli cell tumor.	Seminoma.	Teratoma.	A	Leydig cell tumor.	Leydig cell tumors are stromal tumors that secrete androgens (testosterone), leading to precocious puberty in children or gynecomastia in adults. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End semester 2023	P2, Q11. A 52 years male patient presented by pain and swelling in the right lower abdomen and scrotum, vomiting, distension. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End round 2022	P1, Q1. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End round 2022	P4, Q37. Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct with regard to pyloric obstruction secondary to peptic ulceration?	Pyloric obstruction is suggested by Hypochloremic Hyponatremic Alkalosis.	Pyloric obstruction is suggested by Hypochloremic Hypokalemic Alkalosis. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P9, Q37. The following are true about the inguinal canal except:	Anterior boundary includes external oblique muscle, internal oblique muscle &amp; fascia transversalis. A	Anterior boundary includes external oblique muscle, internal oblique muscle &amp; fascia transversalis. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P9, Q38. The sac of inguinal hernia can be identified by all of the following except:	Containing the vas difference &amp; spermatic artery.	It has defined edges.	Its origin is related to inferior epigastric vessels	Opaque.	Pearly white in color.	A	Containing the vas difference &amp; spermatic artery.	The hernia sac (peritoneum) does not contain the vas and vessels *inside* it; these structures are part of the cord running *alongside* (usually posterior to) the sac.	The sac itself contains viscera (bowel/omentum).	The sac is pearly white.				> Hernia surgery

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P10, Q41. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P11, Q47. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P11, Q48. Postphlebitic limb means:	Phlegmasia alba dolens	Phlegmasia cerulae dolens	Gangrenous limb secondary to DVT	Venous insufficiency secondary to DVT.		D	Venous insufficiency secondary to DVT.	Post-phlebitic (Post-thrombotic) syndrome refers to the chronic venous insufficiency (edema, skin changes, ulcers) resulting from DVT-induced valve damage. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P12, Q49. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P12, Q50. Arterial spasm:	Diagnosed after exploration of the artery	It is a usual pathology of arterial injury	Should be diagnosed before exploration	None of the above.		A	Diagnosed after exploration of the artery	Arterial spasm is a diagnosis of exclusion; mechanical injury or thrombosis must be ruled out (usually by exploration or angio) before attributing ischemia to spasm.	It is dangerous to assume spasm in a trauma patient.	Treat with Papaverine.				> surgery Vascular

Advanced ischemia is indicated by Rest Pain.	ABI &lt; 0.9 is diagnostic; &lt; 0.5 is severe.				> surgery Vascular
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P12, Q52. Gigantism of the limb:	It is with congenital A.V fistula	It is with acquired A.V fistula	It is with false aneurysm	Lymphedema of limb.		A	It is with congenital A.V fistula	Congenital AV fistulas (e.g., Klippel-Trenaunay, Parkes Weber) cause limb hypertrophy (gigantism) due to increased blood flow during growth.	Acquired fistulas occur after trauma (usually adult).	Lymphedema causes swelling, not bony overgrowth.				> surgery Vascular
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P12, Q53. Full thickness burns are characterized by the following except	Presence of blisters.	Brown, black surface.	Leathery look.	Non sensate.		A	Presence of blisters.	Blisters (bullae) are characteristic of Partial Thickness (Second Degree) burns.	Full Thickness (Third Degree) burns are dry, leathery, charred/white, and insensate.	They require grafting.				> surgery trauma

Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P13, Q56. Fastest promoter of epithelialization in wound healing occurs with	Tight approximation of wound edges. B	Occlusive dressing	محلولة في الكتب B<br>Primary intention (suturing/approximation) brings epithelial edges together, allowing epithelialization to bridge the gap within 24-48 hours.	Open wounds require extensive migration and contraction.	Moist dressings help, but approximation is faster.				> Healing surgery Wound
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P13, Q57. All the following statements about sebaceous cyst are true, except:	They are commonest in children.	They do not appear on the palm. Sebaceous (Epidermoid) cysts are common in adults; Dermoid cysts are the common cysts in children (e.g., external angular dermoid).	They are absent on palms/soles (no hair follicles). Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P13, Q58. Drainage of distal pulp space infection is done through:	Bilateral incision of each side of the finger.	Incise the nail fold. D	Short longitudinal skin incision on area of maximum fluctuance not crossing the interphalangeal joint crease. Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P14, Q59. The following statements about fibrocystic breast are all true, except:	May be asymptomatic	May cause nipple retraction. It is a benign condition (ANDI).				> Breast surgery
Surgery MCQ Raihana Med::End module 2024	P14, Q60. The complications and disadvantages of nasogastric tube feeding include all the following except:	A high cost	Possibility of aspiration pneumonia.	Pharyngitis	Nasal ulceration		A	A high cost	Nasogastric feeding is simple and inexpensive compared to TPN or PEG.	Complications include aspiration, sinusitis, pharyngitis, and erosions.
